title: Installing opencv by conda makes pytorch CUDA not available
body: OpenCV python version: 4.6.0 Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 16.04 Python version (conda env): 3.11.5 GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU Driver Version: 465.19.01 After installing opencv by this command though conda, pytorch cuda is not available anymore: ``` conda install -c conda-forge opencv ``` This command replaced the CUDA-enabled pytorch with CPU-enabled-only pytroch: results in: ``` (mouse) $ python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())" False ``` 1. Install pytorch following the official instruction: `conda install pytorch torchvision torchaudio pytorch-cuda=11.7 -c pytorch -c nvidia` 3. Install opencv: `conda install -c conda-forge opencv`

title: Overlapped contours of moments
body:
Hi, I want to know why Contour[0] and Contour[1] are overlapped, is it bug or feature ?
* Contour[0] - Area (M_00) = 18060.00 - Area OpenCV: 18060.00 - Length: 835.76
* Contour[1] - Area (M_00) = 18051.00 - Area OpenCV: 18051.00 - Length: 833.42
![bin](https://i.imgur.com/YKx5LOI.jpg)
![moments](https://i.imgur.com/rqknW6L.png)
```python

import cv2 as cv
import numpy as np
import random as rng


def thresh_callback(val):
    threshold = val
    canny_output = cv.Canny(src_gray, threshold, threshold * 2)
    contours, _ = cv.findContours(
        canny_output, cv.RETR_TREE, cv.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)

    # Get the moments
    mu = [None] * len(contours)
    for i, _ in enumerate(contours):
        mu[i] = cv.moments(contours[i])

    # Get the mass centers
    mc = [None]*len(contours)
    for i, _ in enumerate(contours):
        # add 1e-5 to avoid division by zero
        mc[i] = (mu[i]['m10'] / (mu[i]['m00'] + 1e-5),
                 mu[i]['m01'] / (mu[i]['m00'] + 1e-5))

    # Draw contours
    drawing = np.zeros(
        (canny_output.shape[0], canny_output.shape[1], 3), dtype=np.uint8)

    for i, _ in enumerate(contours):
        color = (rng.randint(0, 256), rng.randint(0, 256), rng.randint(0, 256))
        cv.drawContours(drawing, contours, i, color, 2)
        cv.circle(drawing, (int(mc[i][0]), int(mc[i][1])), 4, color, -1)

        font = cv.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX
        cv.putText(drawing, ' #' + str(i), (int(mc[i][0]), int(mc[i][1])), font,
                   1, (64, 64, 64), 2, cv.LINE_AA)

    cv.imshow('Contours', drawing)

    # Calculate the area with the moments 00 and compare with the result of the OpenCV function
    for i in range(len(contours)):
        print(' * Contour[%d] - Area (M_00) = %.2f - Area OpenCV: %.2f - Length: %.2f' %
              (i, mu[i]['m00'], cv.contourArea(contours[i]), cv.arcLength(contours[i], True)))

    cv.imwrite(path[:4] + '_moments.png', drawing)


path = 'test.jpg'

src = cv.imread(path)
src_gray = cv.cvtColor(src, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
src_gray = cv.blur(src_gray, (3, 3))
source_window = 'Source'
cv.namedWindow(source_window)
cv.imshow(source_window, src)

max_thresh = 255
thresh = 100  # initial threshold
cv.createTrackbar('Canny Thresh:', source_window,
                  thresh, max_thresh, thresh_callback)
thresh_callback(thresh)
cv.waitKey()


```
It's not always like that, more complex examples are overlapped about 90% of the all contours.

title: Confusion about opencv's distortion model w.r.t Brown–Conrady model
body:
As described in https://docs.opencv.org/4.4.0/d4/d94/tutorial_camera_calibration.html:
x_{distorted} = x( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
y_{distorted} = y( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
x_{distorted} = x + [ 2p_1xy + p_2(r^2+2x^2)]
y_{distorted} = y + [ p_1(r^2+ 2y^2)+ 2p_2xy]
But in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distortion_(optics), Brown–Conrady model:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8348898/96449200-189f4080-1247-11eb-8d1a-a4366a6260b9.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8348898/96449247-25239900-1247-11eb-8488-55ae3c911354.png)
the above two models are in two different directions. I'm pretty confused when being told that OpenCV uses Brown–Conrady model for distortion.

title: modifiedLaplacian Same code working in C++ ... not working in Python
body:
opencv-python 4.2.0.32
```
import cv2
import glob
import numpy as np

def modifiedLaplacian(img):
    ''''LAPM' algorithm (Nayar89)'''
    M = np.array([-1, 2, -1])
    G = cv2.getGaussianKernel(ksize=3, sigma=-1)
    Lx = cv2.sepFilter2D(src=img, ddepth=cv2.CV_64F, kernelX=M, kernelY=G)
    Ly = cv2.sepFilter2D(src=img, ddepth=cv2.CV_64F, kernelX=G, kernelY=M)
    FM = np.abs(Lx) + np.abs(Ly)
    return cv2.mean(FM)[0]

mylist = glob.glob("/home/ashutosh/Desktop/blur_dataset/images/*.jpg")
for item in mylist:
	crop_img = cv2.imread(item)
	lm = modifiedLaplacian(crop_img)
	print(item+"========"+str(lm))
```
Output
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "is_blur.py", line 17, in <module>
    lm = modifiedLaplacian(crop_img)
  File "is_blur.py", line 9, in modifiedLaplacian
    Lx = cv2.sepFilter2D(src=img, ddepth=cv2.CV_64F, kernelX=M, kernelY=G)
cv2.error: OpenCV(4.2.0) /io/opencv/modules/imgproc/src/filter.dispatch.cpp:1450: error: (-215:Assertion failed) kernelX.type() == kernelY.type() && (kernelX.cols == 1 || kernelX.rows == 1) && (kernelY.cols == 1 || kernelY.rows == 1) in function 'sepFilter2D'
```
opencv-c++ | latest github
```
double modifiedLaplacian(const cv::Mat& src)
{
        cv::Mat M = (cv::Mat_<double>(3, 1) << -1, 2, -1);
        cv::Mat G = cv::getGaussianKernel(3, -1, CV_64F);

        cv::Mat Lx;
        cv::sepFilter2D(src, Lx, CV_64F, M, G);

        cv::Mat Ly;
        cv::sepFilter2D(src, Ly, CV_64F, G, M);

        cv::Mat FM = cv::abs(Lx) + cv::abs(Ly);

        double focusMeasure = cv::mean(FM).val[0];
        return focusMeasure;
}
```
This is working fine !

title: imgcodecs: Support Quite OK Image format(QOI)
body:
Motivation
Quite OK Image format ( ".qoi" ) is the faster lossless compression format.
Reference implementation is released with MIT license.
Details is https://github.com/phoboslab/qoi
I tried to support QOI encoder/decoder in imgcodecs.
It seems to be faster than libpng about 3x.
Test result
Test result on i7-4790S (VMware, Ubuntu 23.10)
- Image is 3840x2160x3
- QOI(System) is used with apt-package.
- QOI(Modified) is custom version, which is contained into 3rdparty.
| Format | Method | mean time(ms) | Comment |
| PNG           | Decode |  74.14 | x1.0 |
| QOI(System)   | Decode |  22.59 | x3.3 |
| QOI(Modified) | Decode |  22.59 | x3.3 |
| PNG           | Encode | 108.79 | x1.0 |
| QOI(System)   | Encode |  29.86 | x3.6 |
| QOI(Modified) | Encode |  24.05 | x4.5 |
Limitation
- QOI doesn't support single channel image(it supports only CV_8UC3 or CV_8UC4)
- QOI Reference implementation seems no version information.
```plaintext
QOI(System)
[ RUN      ] CodecsCommon.Decode/6, where GetParam() = (1, 3840x2160, ".png")

title: cvInitMatNDHeader in cvHistogram strange step sizes
body:
The post on answers didnt get any replys so im trying it here.
I am trying to create a Histogram using the CreateHistogram funktion, so far so good.
When i checked into the memory there lies a flat CvMatND which in turn has a size and a step for each dimension.
As an example a 3D Mat with sizes of 5 results in (5,100,5,20,5,4).
Checking in the code the funktion goes like: (Cut out unimportant parts)
```
int64 step = CV_ELEM_SIZE(type); //step = get element size 
for( int i = dims - 1; i >= 0; i-- ) // running through all dims from the back
mat->dim[i].step = (int)step; // puts step into the Mat
step *= sizes[i]; //multiplies with size now
```
this is strange for 2 reasons.
1. the last step is always the size of one element, this should imho definetly not be like that.
2. the step grow, this CAN be correct if you define it so that the dimensions encompass all lower dimensions.
Is this a bug or does it have a deeper meanig i dont understand?

```
import csv
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os
  
def video_to_csv(name, num_frames):
    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(name)
    i = 0
    NUMFRAMES = num_frames
    name = os.path.splitext(name)[0]
    while(True):
        if i >= NUMFRAMES:
            break
        ret, frame = cap.read()
        if frame.all():

        with open(name + str(i) + '.csv', 'w', newline='') as csvfile:

title: Different weights for dnn's face detector
body:
Hi all,
I am playing with the face detector code, but I have some bad results when it comes to faces that have a small size in the image because they are far from the source. In [this article](https://towardsdatascience.com/face-detection-models-which-to-use-and-why-d263e82c302c) i read that this issue can be improved by passing as input to the "blobFromImage" function the original size of the image, instead of resizing it to 300x300.
My problem when doing this is that, at the time of getting back the predictions, in some cases they have values greater than 1, so it overflows the image's dimension. Because of the link I previously mentioned, I was wondering if there are other weights anywhere that are not the "res10_300x300_ssd_iter_140000.caffemodel", as I understand this have been trained for working with 300x300 images. Do these weights exist anywhere else? Is there anything else that I should modify instead of using another pretrained weights? Or anything else that I am missing?
Thank you for enlightening me.

title: video stream stiching ,error : stiching failed status :3
body:
OpenCV version: 4.8.0
Operating System / Platform: window 11
Python version:3.11.5
import cv2
import numpy as np
def find_homography(img1, img2, ransac_threshold=5.0):
orb = cv2.ORB_create(nfeatures=1000)
kp1, des1 = orb.detectAndCompute(img1, None)
kp2, des2 = orb.detectAndCompute(img2, None)
bf = cv2.BFMatcher(cv2.NORM_HAMMING, crossCheck=True)
matches = bf.match(des1, des2)
matches = sorted(matches, key=lambda x: x.distance)
src_pts = np.float32([kp1[m.queryIdx].pt for m in matches]).reshape(-1, 1, 2)
dst_pts = np.float32([kp2[m.trainIdx].pt for m in matches]).reshape(-1, 1, 2)
homography, _ = cv2.findHomography(src_pts, dst_pts, cv2.RANSAC, ransac_threshold)
return homography
def stitch_frames(stitcher, cap):
# Initialize the homography matrix with the identity matrix
homography = np.eye(3)
# Read the first frame from the camera to initialize the homography matrix
ret, frame_prev = cap.read()
if not ret:
print("Error: Could not read frame from the camera.")
cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
exit()
# Resize the first frame if needed
frame_prev = cv2.resize(frame_prev, (640, 480))
cv2.namedWindow("Stitched Result", cv2.WINDOW_NORMAL)
while True:
# Capture frames from the camera
ret, frame = cap.read()
if not ret:
break
# Resize the frame if needed
frame = cv2.resize(frame, (640, 480))
# Update the homography matrix based on the current and previous frames
homography = find_homography(frame_prev, frame)
# Manually set the homography (replace with your own matrix)
# For example, use the identity matrix:
homography = np.eye(3)
# Use the updated homography matrix for stitching
status, result = stitcher.stitch([frame_prev, frame], homography)
if status == cv2.Stitcher_OK:
cv2.imshow("Stitched Result", result)
else:
print(f"Stitching failed with status: {status}")
# Set the current frame as the previous frame for the next iteration
frame_prev = frame.copy()
if cv2.waitKey(1) == ord('q'):
break
cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
if __name__ == "__main__":
# Create a Stitcher object
stitcher = cv2.Stitcher_create()
# Create a VideoCapture object for the camera (use 0 for the default camera)
cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
# Check if the camera opened successfully
if not cap.isOpened():
print("Error: Could not open camera.")
exit()
# Stitch frames using the Stitcher class and homography matrix
stitch_frames(stitcher, cap)
"""if the environment changes significantly between frames, it can affect the stitching process. Ensure that the scene remains relatively constant during the capture."""

title: supporting protobuf v22 and later(with abseil-cpp/C++17)
body:
Hello, The original issue is https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/23466#issuecomment-1748936827.
Related https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/23791 (I'm sorry if this is duplicated.)
What  is problem
When using external(not built-in) protobuf v22 and later, building dnn module is failed.
Reason
Because protobuf v22 has a few breaking changes. ( https://protobuf.dev/support/migration/#cpp-22 )
- it drops supporting C++11.
- it depends abseil-cpp
- And std::string_view in abseil-cpp seems to request C++17 and later.
How to fix it ?
I believe.
- keeping internal protobuf (v19) is better to support C++11.
- Updating to v21 is better, but evenif updated, we can use it only until 2024/Q1 with supporting. ( https://protobuf.dev/support/version-support/ )
- After 2024/Q2, we must be update internal protobuf(e.g. v24 LTS) with abseil-cpp.
- The abseil configuration maybe relax C++11 supporting (https://github.com/abseil/abseil-cpp/blob/master/absl/base/options.h).
- supporintg external protobuf needs some limitation to avoid some compile error.
- If using v22 and later, abseil-cpp and C++17 are required.

title: A formula of official documentation and source code are different
body:
**Hello,**
**I have a question about the formula in the official documentation.**
**You can find the formula in ink below:**
[https://docs.opencv.org/4.5.4/db/d58/group__calib3d__fisheye.html](url)
**This is the formula about distorted point coordinates conversion into pixel coordinates:**
x′=(θd/r)a                    (1)
y′=(θd/r)b                    (2)
u=fx(x′+αy′)+cx           (3)
v=fyy′+cy                     (4)
**I found the source code in link below:**
[https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/4.x/modules/calib3d/src/fisheye.cpp](url)
**And this how the source code programed:**
double scale = (r == 0) ? 1.0 : theta_d / r;
u = f[0]*x*scale + c[0];
v = f[1]*y*scale + c[1];

title: Converting from RGB => YUV => YUYV => RGB results in different image
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.0.0
- Operating System / Platform => Manjaro Linux 64 Bit
- Compiler => Python 3.7.1
I have an image dataset stored as RGB images. I need to convert these RGB images to YUYV images. While it is possible to convert images from YUYV (also known as YUV422) into RGB via COLOR_YUV2RGB_YUYV, there is no reverse operation for this. I thought it should be simple to implement this with the YUV conversion and subsampling, but when I attempted that and afterwards converted the image back to RGB, it looks slightly different and more saturated.
![difference](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5030127/55040377-61215880-5028-11e9-85b2-51952222ec93.png)
I used [this sample image](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/opencv/opencv/master/samples/data/home.jpg). This snipped is a stand alone working sample of what I did and what seems to go wrong.
```
import cv2
import numpy as np

# Load sample image
img_bgr = cv2.imread("home.jpg")
cv2.imshow("original", img_bgr)
cv2.waitKey(0)

# Convert from BGR to YUV
img_yuv = cv2.cvtColor(img_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2YUV)

# Converting directly back from YUV to BGR results in an (almost) identical image
img_bgr_restored = cv2.cvtColor(img_yuv, cv2.COLOR_YUV2BGR)
cv2.imshow("converted", img_bgr_restored)
cv2.waitKey(0)
diff = img_bgr.astype(np.int16) - img_bgr_restored
print("mean/stddev diff (BGR => YUV => BGR)", np.mean(diff), np.std(diff))

# Create YUYV from YUV
y0 = np.expand_dims(img_yuv[...,0][::,::2], axis=2)
u = np.expand_dims(img_yuv[...,1][::,::2], axis=2)
y1 = np.expand_dims(img_yuv[...,0][::,1::2], axis=2)
v = np.expand_dims(img_yuv[...,2][::,::2], axis=2)
img_yuyv = np.concatenate((y0, u, y1, v), axis=2)
img_yuyv_cvt = img_yuyv.reshape(img_yuyv.shape[0], img_yuyv.shape[1] * 2, int(img_yuyv.shape[2] / 2))

# Convert back to BGR results in more saturated image.
img_bgr_restored = cv2.cvtColor(img_yuyv_cvt, cv2.COLOR_YUV2BGR_YUYV)
cv2.imshow("converted", img_bgr_restored)
cv2.waitKey(0)

diff = img_bgr.astype(np.int16) - img_bgr_restored
print("mean/stddev diff (BGR => YUV => YUYV => BGR)", np.mean(diff), np.std(diff))
```
Output:
```
mean/stddev diff (BGR => YUV => BGR) 0.044821845160590276 0.539779183532531
mean/stddev diff (BGR => YUV => YUYV => BGR) -0.3019137912326389 11.760396080102238
```
The high standard deviation shows that the resulting image differs quiet significantly from the original image. I would expect this to be a lot lower, in the order of converting to and from plain YUV.

void HOGDescriptor::computeGradient(const Mat& img, Mat& grad, Mat& qangle,
Size paddingTL, Size paddingBR) const
After calculating the gradient Dx,Dy,we need to convert the to magnitude and angle.

cartToPolar( Dx, Dy, Mag, Angle, false );

for( ; x < width; x++ )

From here,I know you must take the variable Angle is in range [-pi/2,pi/2],

title: Shared mat and isSubmatrix()
body:
For performances reasons, I often need to know if a matrix is referencing another one or is self-contained. If it is a reference, I must release() it before using it as a temporary matrix to store results.
So far, I query the ->u->refcount field, but I am unhappy with such a hack.
I though that using isSubmatrix() would be the solution, but it is not.
```
  cv::Mat org(cv::Size(1280, 1024), CV_32FC1);
  cv::Mat roi(org, cv::Rect(0, 0, 10, 10));
  cv::Mat ref_org = org;
  cv::Mat ref_roi = roi;
  printf("org.isSubmatrix : %d\r\n", org.isSubmatrix()); //-> NO
  printf("roi.isSubmatrix : %d\r\n", roi.isSubmatrix());//-> YES
  printf("ref_org.isSubmatrix : %d\r\n", ref_org.isSubmatrix());//-> NO, I expected YES as a "full frame submatrix"
  printf("ref_roi.isSubmatrix : %d\r\n", ref_roi.isSubmatrix());//-> YES
```
Is there any reason why ref_org is not considered a subMatrix ? This could make sense for my problem.

title: Issues with recognition whilst using IP Stream only
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.1
- Operating System / Platform => Linux
I will try ask in here as got no response on the forum. I have been working with OpenCV in an application since last year. The first version I was capturing the frame from a webcam and using Haarcascades and without an issue it would recognise a face nearly every time.
I came into some issues with getting a stable web based stream going, after trying multiple solutions I moved to a new way. This is still using the exact same webcam except Linux Motion is accessing it and OpenCV is now connecting to the mjpeg stream from Linux Motion through a secure Nginx server, the stream is on the same device as the OpenCV script.
Since doing this the quality of the stream has increased massively, but, it now no longer detects faces, at all hardly, I have compared screen shots of frames from when OpenCV was accessing the webcam and frames from when OpenCV is accessing the stream, and apart from the improved quality of the frames there really is not any difference, yet OpenCV refuses to identify a face, it is literally a case where I have to move the camera around and hold a position to identify a face, before I could be walking past on the other side of the room and it would detect my face.
After trying everything i could think of and find on Google, I went back to the webcam, instantly it was detecting my face in whatever position, in what ever light. I have tried multiple other ways of streaming to the web again but still not successful so have moved back to the Motion stream again to try work this out.
Can anyone shed any light on this, it does not make sense to me that an improvement in quality suddenly breaks facial identification. I have tried playing with the frame settings, resolution, contrast, hue, brightness and nothing I can do seems to work.
```
self.OpenCVCapture.open('http://MOTION_STREAM_ADDRESS/stream.mjpg')
self.OpenCVCapture.set(5, 30) 
self.OpenCVCapture.set(3,1280)
self.OpenCVCapture.set(4,720)
self.OpenCVCapture.set(10,1)
```

title: Warp image with optical flow to produce a new image
body:
I tried asking in the forum, but it seems pretty much inactive. I am not sure what I am doing wrong, but the code below doesn't generate the expected output. I have `im1` and `im2` (attached below) for which I compute the optical flow. Then I want to apply the flow to `im1` to double check that it produces `im2`:
![0001](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3345261/37369136-79851124-26c5-11e8-9820-685d6a636caf.png)
![0002](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3345261/37369145-7cdaf9c4-26c5-11e8-9d87-c2048be99267.png)
```python
import cv2
import numpy as np
from imageio import imread

im1 = np.asarray(imread("0001.png"))
im2 = np.asarray(imread("0002.png"))
im1 = np.float64(im1 / 255)
im2 = np.float64(im2 / 255)

flow = cv2.calcOpticalFlowFarneback(im1, im2, None, 0.5, 3, 15, 3, 5, 1.2, 0)

def draw_hsv(flow):
    h, w = flow.shape[:2]
    fx, fy = flow[:,:,0], flow[:,:,1]
    ang = np.arctan2(fy, fx) + np.pi
    v = np.sqrt(fx*fx+fy*fy)
    hsv = np.zeros((h, w, 3), np.uint8)
    hsv[...,0] = ang*(180/np.pi/2)
    hsv[...,1] = 255
    hsv[...,2] = cv2.normalize(v, None, 0, 255, cv2.NORM_MINMAX)
    bgr = cv2.cvtColor(hsv, cv2.COLOR_HSV2BGR)
    return bgr

def warp_flow(img, flow):
    h, w = flow.shape[:2]
    flow = -flow
    flow[:,:,0] += np.arange(w)
    flow[:,:,1] += np.arange(h)[:,np.newaxis]
    res = cv2.remap(img, flow, None, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)
    return res

hsv = draw_hsv(flow)
im2w = warp_flow(im1*255, flow)
cv2.imwrite("/tmp/flow.jpg",hsv)
cv2.imwrite("/tmp/im1.jpg", im1*255)
cv2.imwrite("/tmp/im2.jpg", im2*255)
cv2.imwrite("/tmp/im2w.jpg", im2w)
```
The printed images do not look correct. Could you please provide some help on how to fix this use case?

title: Feature descripency in findEssentialMat
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.5.0
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 20.04 64 bit
- Compiler => Installed from conda forge
The `findEssentialMatrix` function in 4.5.0 and above does not expose the `maxIters` argument as in the earlier versions.
API in 3.4.15:
```
cv::findEssentialMat (InputArray points1, InputArray points2, double focal, Point2d pp, int method, double prob, double threshold, int maxIters, OutputArray mask=noArray())
```
This API is missing in 4.5.0. There is no version of `findEssentialMat` which has the maxIters argument exposed.
Related issue where this argument was actually exposed: https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/19845.
The lack of `maxIters` argument is causing regression in performance where I was using a high number of `maxIters`.
- [ x] I report the issue, it's not a question
- [ x] I checked the problem with documentation, FAQ, open issues,
forum.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found solution
- [x ] There is reproducer code and related data files: videos, images, onnx, etc

title: Sobel XandY-combining for performance
body:
Usually/often both Sobel X and Y are needed together (considered usecase here).
This applies to all Arch (e.g. ARM, x86, x64, OpenCL, CUDA, ..), if ImageSize > L1-Cache.
Principal idea is to go from image pipelining (finish each operator, before next one starts) to row-pipelining (each row enters L1 only once). General benefits: much faster (with typical L1 cache sizes) + less lines of code + less temporal memory.
Edge-detection is a major method in CV in general, and Sobel is taken here as example.
The Issue applies to both Python and C-Interface
This Interface Code should only explain the idea, it is not intended to be final.
# 1. the grayscale case (only 1 channel) -- [RGB/BGR see below]
ddepth = cv2.CV_16S # no important now
gray = cv2.imread(image, CV_LOAD_IMAGE_GRAYSCALE) # cv2.IMREAD_COLOR
grad_x = cv2.Sobel(gray, ddepth, 1, 0, ksize=3, scale=1, delta=0, borderType=cv2.BORDER_DEFAULT)
grad_y = cv2.Sobel(gray, ddepth, 0, 1, ksize=3, scale=1, delta=0, borderType=cv2.BORDER_DEFAULT)
# a typical Image (8bit, 1..8 Megapixels) fits nowadays into L3-cache (some Megabytes), but not into L1-cache (32..100 KB)
# if you calculate both Sobels successively, SobelY still works from L3
# but if you would work in in parallel (e.g. Line1_gx, Line1_gy, Line2_gx, Line2_gy, ..) you would gain L1-speed for GradY
grad_x,grad_y = SobelGradBoth(gray, ksize) # possible python syntax
# 2. grayscale cases, but combinations of Sobel X+Y
# 2a) grad = abs(grad_x) + abs(grad_y)
abs_grad_x = cv.convertScaleAbs(grad_x)
abs_grad_y = cv.convertScaleAbs(grad_y)
grad = cv.addWeighted(abs_grad_x, 0.5, abs_grad_y, 0.5, 0)
grad8= cv.addWeighted(abs_grad_x, 0.25, abs_grad_y, 0.25, 0) # (in CV_8U) even better, as pixel-bandwidth limits CV
# a single optimized call would merge 5 runs (2x Sobel, 2x convertScaleAbs, 1x addWeighted) into one (and gain L1-speed for 4 of them)
grad = GradSumFromSobels(gray, ksize, OUT_FORMAT) # new possible syntax
# 2b) thres_8U = threshold(dx^2 + dy^2, thresh*thresh, 255, cv.THRESH_BINARY)
# same here, including extra 16 to 8 bit method (thres_8U is similar to Canny without NMS)
# 2c) Magnitude^2 = (dx^2 + dy^2)
mag2_16 = Magn2FromSobels(dx^2 + dy^2) # 16S or 16U
mag_scl8U  = Magn8FromSobels(dx^2 + dy^2) / 512 # (255^2 + 255^2) /510 = 255, 512=2^9
# same here = many operations together (6 vs 1)
# 2d) mag = sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2) (using cv::magnitude, but caution sqrt is slow)
mag_32F = Magn32F_FromSobels(dx^2 + dy^2)
mag_8F  = Magn8F_FromSobels(dx^2 + dy^2) # Minifloat, less memory + faster sqrt
# same here (and even more improvments thinkable by avoiding sqrt)
# like: log2_mag = log2(dx^2 + dy^2) * 0.5 # log2( CV_16U ) = __builtin_clz() or LZCNT()
# benefit: fast (no sqrt), similar information (rounded), output could be CV_8U (skip /2)
# optional: direction bits (part of 8U byte), often direction information can be rough (strong rounded)
# 2d1) 1 bit direction: bit = abs(dx) > abs(dy)  [e.g. bit array 7+1]
# 2d2) 2 bit direction: bit1= sign(dx), bit2 = sign(dy) // a fast atan2 with maxerror=45deg
# 3. Color images, RGB/BGR (3 channel)
# single direction, gain = 9 : 1
sobel_sum_x = abs(grad_red_x) + abs(grad_green_x) + abs(grad_blue_x) = SobelRgbX(img,ksize) # out = 16U & 8U [scaled /1024]
sobel_sum_y = abs(grad_red_y) + abs(grad_green_y) + abs(grad_blue_y) = SobelRgbY(img,ksize) # out = 16U & 8U [scaled /1024]
# both direction, gain = 19 : 1  !!!!
grad_rgb = sobel_sum_x + sobel_sum_y = SobelRgbXandY(img,ksize) # output = 16U & 8U [scaled /2048 or /(6.0*255) ]
mag2_rgb = Magn2_FromSobelRgb(dx^2 + dy^2) # output = 16U
magT_rgb = MagnT_FromSobelRgb(dx^2 + dy^2 > thresh*thresh) # THRESH_BINARY

title: Fix Memory Leak in cv::resize Function in OpenCV 4.10.0
body:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from __future__ import print_function
import sys
import os
import unittest
from contextlib import contextmanager
import logging

# Set up logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Python 3 moved urlopen to urllib.requests
try:
    from urllib.request import urlopen  # Python 3
except ImportError:
    from urllib import urlopen  # Python 2

basedir = os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__))
sys.dont_write_bytecode = True  # Don't generate .pyc files / __pycache__ directories
sys.path.append(os.path.join(os.path.split(basedir)[0], "modules", "python", "test"))
from tests_common import NewOpenCVTests

@contextmanager
def modified_sys_path(directory):
    if directory not in sys.path:
        sys.path.append(directory)
        yield
        sys.path.remove(directory)
    else:
        yield

def discover_tests(loader, tests, directory, pattern):
    """Helper function to discover and add tests from a specific directory."""
    logger.info(f"Discovering python tests from: {directory}")
    with modified_sys_path(directory):
        discovered_tests = loader.discover(directory, pattern=pattern, top_level_dir=directory)
        logger.info(f"    found {discovered_tests.countTestCases()} tests")
        tests.addTests(discovered_tests)

def load_tests(loader, tests, pattern):
    cwd = os.getcwd()
    config_file = 'opencv_apps_python_tests.cfg'
    locations = [cwd, basedir]
    
    if os.path.exists(config_file):
        with open(config_file, 'r') as f:
            additional_locations = [str(s).strip() for s in f.readlines()]
            if additional_locations:
                locations += additional_locations
    else:
        logger.warning(f'OpenCV tests config file ({config_file}) is missing, running subset of tests')

    tests_pattern = os.environ.get('OPENCV_APPS_TEST_FILTER', 'test_*') + '.py'
    if tests_pattern != 'test_*.py':
        logger.info(f'Tests filter: {tests_pattern}')

    processed = set()
    for l in locations:
        l = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(cwd, l)) if not os.path.isabs(l) else l
        if l in processed:
            continue
        processed.add(l)
        discover_tests(loader, tests, l, tests_pattern)

    return tests

if __name__ == '__main__':
    NewOpenCVTests.bootstrap()
```
'''Issue Description:
The OpenCV function cv::resize was found to have a potential memory leak in certain versions (e.g., OpenCV 4.10.0). When resizing images, memory usage could increase without being properly released, leading to performance issues and excessive memory consumption over time, especially when processing large or multiple images in a loop.
Fix Implemented:
The code was refactored to explicitly release memory associated with OpenCV matrices (cv::Mat) after they are no longer needed.
After resizing the image and writing the output to a file, both the resized_image and input_image matrices are explicitly released using .release() to free up memory.
This ensures that memory is properly deallocated after the resizing operation, preventing memory leaks.'''
1. Error Handling for Imports:
You can handle the ImportError more cleanly by using try-except at import time. Instead of catching the error during runtime, it's more readable to use a cleaner fallback approach.

except ImportError:
from urllib import urlopen  # Python 2
2. Refactor Repeated Code:
You have code for discovering tests that is repeated. You can abstract it into a helper function to reduce redundancy:
python
Copy code
def discover_tests(loader, tests, directory, pattern):
"""Helper function to discover and add tests from a specific directory."""
print(f"Discovering python tests from: {directory}")
sys_path_modify = directory not in sys.path
if sys_path_modify:
sys.path.append(directory)  # Hack python loader
discovered_tests = loader.discover(directory, pattern=pattern, top_level_dir=directory)
print(f"    found {discovered_tests.countTestCases()} tests")
tests.addTests(discovered_tests)
if sys_path_modify:
sys.path.remove(directory)
You can call this helper function in your main test-loading loop.
3. Use with for Opening Files:
Ensure you're using the context manager (with) to open files (which you already did). This makes it easier to handle file closing, even in case of exceptions.

title: connectedComponents merges regions separated by single pixel divider
body:
##### System information (version)
- opencv-python => 4.1
- Operating System => macos 10.14.4 Mojave
- Python => 3.7
It appears that `connectedComponents` does not separate components that are divided by a single pixel dividing line.
For context, this is an issue when trying to obtain region stats from an image segmented with the `watershed` method (ultimately I want to use `connectedComponentsWithStats`). Since `connectedComponents` does not handle already labeled images, I binarize the image by setting the background and dividing lines as 0 and the labeled regions as 255. The resulting binary image (`segmented_bin`) has regions separated by a single-pixel-wide line.
![CoinsSegmented](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8866213/57786871-499a4e00-7702-11e9-8915-3b0ff10955a1.JPG)
Running `connectedComponents` on this image returns only 2 regions: the background and an aggregate of the foreground regions.
Here is a sample code to reproduce the issue
```python
import cv2

[...] # Prepare markers as in https://docs.opencv.org/3.4/d3/db4/tutorial_py_watershed.html

segmented = cv2.watershed(img,markers)

segmented_bin = segmented.copy()
segmented_bin[segmented < 2] = 0 # -1 is dividing regions, no 0s, 1 is background
segmented_bin[segmented > 1] = 255 # all above 1 are distinct regions
num_labels, label_image = cv2.connectedComponents(segmented_bin.astype('uint8'), 8, cv2.CV_16U, cv2.CCL_GRANA)
```
Executing this code returns `num_labels = 2` instead of 27.

title: ANN_MLP - large dataset results in stack overflow and other errors
macOS 15: cv2.VideoCapture Incompatibility with Continuity Camera
body:
Solution 1: Modify Info.plist
macOS requires permission for Continuity Camera access in the app's Info.plist. You can add the necessary key-value pairs to your Info.plist file:
Add the following keys in Info.plist:
xml
Copy code
<key>NSCameraUsageDescription</key>
<string>Your app requires camera access</string>
<key>NSCameraUseContinuityCameraDeviceType</key>
<true/>
This grants access to the camera and Continuity Camera on macOS 15+.
Solution 2: Specify AVFoundation Backend in OpenCV
OpenCV’s cv2.VideoCapture can explicitly specify a backend, which may help on macOS:
python
Copy code
import cv2
# Use the AVFoundation backend for better macOS compatibility
webcam_video_stream = cv2.VideoCapture(0, cv2.CAP_AVFOUNDATION)
if not webcam_video_stream.isOpened():
print("Error: Unable to access the camera")
else:
print("Camera accessed successfully")
# You can proceed with your video capture code
Solution 3: Update OpenCV with a Patch (if Available)
This is an issue with OpenCV’s compatibility with newer macOS versions, so you may need to:
Check for updates to OpenCV, as a new release might have addressed this.
Build OpenCV from source with updated settings if OpenCV releases an official fix.

title: No UMat support in functions split() and merge()
body:
Unlike many other functions, the two functions split() and merge() do not support the use of the UMat class.
It is not possible to split a three channel UMat into an array (or vector) of three UMat directly without using the method getMat().
The output (second argument) can't be an array of UMat.
Same applies to mixChannels()

title: VideoCapture - Can we use device names instead of numbers?
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
I'm using OpenCV and Python on a Raspberry Pi  to read streams from 3 Cameras using the VideoCapture object. The cameras might be removed and reattached from time to time, thus resulting in different videodevices (/dev/videox) for the same cameras now and then. My code cannot be adjusted everytime, so I cannot be certain which device number to pass to the VideoCapture object.
I usually use Udev rules to create symbolic links to the real devices, however, OpenCV only accepts device numbers and no paths to actual devices.
People usually have to resort to a collection of hacks to get the right device number. I, for example, read the target of my simlink (e.g. /dev/video6) and take the last character (6) as device number.
Is there any strong reason against using device names on linux systems? If no, would that be possible?

title: Using OpenCV library in java project
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.1
- Operating System / Platform => Linux, Debian 8, Ubuntu 16.04
- Compiler => g, g++
I'm using OpenCV library in one of my projects for face recognition and motion detection. The outcome is a jar file that I can successfully execute on my system. The problem starts when other people try to execute the same jar in their environment. The OpenCV library can't be recognized on their system and the only way to make it work is to compile new OpenCV jar and .so files and add them to the project.
My question here is whether there is a way to compile the OpenCV jar and .so files that could be used on any platform as part of my project.

title: contourArea does not produce a correct number
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.3.1 (python)
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 64 Bit
- Compiler => Visual Studio 2017
I had created 2 boxes both with an area of 500.000 pixels but with different dimensions. Using OpenCV findContours I got the contours, but calculating the area of these boxes resulted in 49551 and 49401.
The contours where correctly detected.
The first contour is 200 by 250 and gives a result of 49.551 (199 * 249?).
The second contour is 100 by 500 and gives a result of 49.401 (99 * 499?)
My hypothesis is the (upper or bottom) and (left  or right) border is not taken in to account.
`import cv2
import numpy as np
contour1 = np.array([[[  0,   0]],
[[  0, 199]],
[[249, 199]],
[[249,   0]]], dtype=np.int32)
contour2 = np.array([[[  0,   0]],
[[  0, 99]],
[[499, 99]],
[[499,   0]]], dtype=np.int32)
print("contour 1, 200 by 250 has size", cv2.contourArea(contour1))
print("contour 2, 100 by 500 has size", cv2.contourArea(contour2))`
###### PS
- I do not know if the problem is also present in C++
- The number is close to the correct answer but becomes a problem with smaller contours (a 10x10 square is off by 19%)

title: cv::resize memory leak
Installing fails on Raspberry Pi Zero 2 running Raspberry Pi OS lite 64 bit.
body:
OpenCV version: 4.9.0
Operating System / Platform: Raspberry Pi OS lite 64bit on Raspberry Pi Zero 2
Compiler & compiler version: GCC 12.2.0
CMake version: CMake 3.25.1
Im installing OpenCV from source on the raspberry pi zero 2. The build proccess works fine, but when installing via "checkinstall make install" It fails to install by saying the final destination is not a directory. The odd thing is when doing the exact same thing on the 32bit OS version the installation works just fine. This is only on the 64bit version.
I need it to work on the 64 Bit version as the other stuff requires the 64 Bit version.
This issue is still there on the newest OpenCV version too.
Error message:
```
-- Install configuration: "Release"
CMake Error at cmake_install.cmake:46 (file):
  file INSTALL destination: /usr/local/share/licenses/opencv4 is not a
  directory.
```
Here are the exact steps to reproduce:
Flash SD card with Raspberry Pi OS lite (64 bit) via raspberry pi imager.
SSH into the pi over WiFi.
Update:
```
sudo apt-get -y update
```
Get required packages:
```
sudo apt-get -y install cmake git build-essential pkg-config checkinstall libcamera-dev libopencv-dev libjpeg-dev libpng-dev libtiff-dev libprotobuf-dev protobuf-compiler libv4l-dev libavcodec-dev libavformat-dev libswscale-dev libgtk2.0-dev
```
Increase SWAP size:
```
sudo sed -i 's/CONF_SWAPSIZE=100/CONF_SWAPSIZE=2048/g' /etc/dphys-swapfile
sudo /etc/init.d/dphys-swapfile restart
```
Clone OpenCV:
```mkdir OpenCV
cd OpenCV
git clone https://github.com/opencv/opencv.git --branch 4.9.0
```
Build OpenCV:
```mkdir build
cd build
cmake -DENABLE_NEON=ON -DBUILD_TESTS=OFF -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_EXAMPLES=OFF -DBUILD_opencv_apps=OFF -DBUILD_DOCS=OFF -DBUILD_PERF_TESTS=OFF -DWITH_TBB=ON -DWITH_OPENMP=ON -DWITH_IPP=OFF -DENABLE_FAST_MATH=ON ../opencv
make -j 2
```
Install OpenCV:
```
sudo checkinstall make install
```
This step is ultimately what fails on the 64 Bit OS version but is fine on the 32 Bit version.

title: DNN_TARGET_CUDA - Multiple GPU configuration
body:

class Application:
def __init__(self, output_path = "./"):
""" Initialize application which with OpenCV """
self.output_path = output_path  # store output path

title: Make BarcodeDetector resolution invariant
body:
The ```cv::barcode::BarcodeDetector``` relies on hardcoded parameters in ```bardetect.cpp```.
The barcode localisation algorithm is very good, but it has some limitations in terms of high-res and small barcode inputs that make it both resolution and barcode scale **dependent**.
I had managed to figure out four values that impact this:
- the input image is downsampled to 512px by default (a workaround is to use ```np.lib.stride_tricks.sliding_window_view``` to process the image in patches, and NMS the resulting boxes afterwards which is inefficient).
- the gradient magnitude threshold is permanently set to 64.
- window sizes for filters have been empirically determined with respect to input size and is not possible to adjust them ```{0.01f, 0.03f, 0.06f, 0.08f}``` for other scenarios.
- the NMS Box threshold is calculated and set for a 512px wide image
There are a lot more parameters in ```bardetect.cpp``` but these are the most relevant ones which need to be tuned to improve the barcode localisation performance.
This is what the stock detector returns for a 2048x1536 input image.
![detector_params_fail](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/69245609/2062fd83-1bc8-45cd-aa3f-e3e50045df6c)
Obviously this is very poor performance. But after manually adjusting these parameters, I am able to successfully localise each barcode in the image.
![detector_params_success](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/69245609/92369a2d-617d-467e-8255-1b95a29a141d)
Proposal on how to set detector parameters, and reference values used for the test pattern.
```
detector = cv2.barcode.BarcodeDetector()

div = float(np.max(frame.shape)) / 512.
detector.setDownSampleThresh( div * 512 ) # prevent downsample
detector.setDetectorScales( [ 0.01/div, 0.03/div, 0.06/div, 0.08/div ] )
detector.setGradientThresh( 256 )
```
This should keep compatibility and maintain the current behaviour of the standard barcode detector.
**Reference image**
![testpattern](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/69245609/c471921c-80b0-4791-b654-674a3bd51ee1)
LE: Updated proposal to set detector parameters per 24903

title: log a debug message if a capture backend is generally available but isn't capabable of either capture by index or capture by filename
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
I am working on a few issues pertaining hw accelerated videoio and I experimented with the different backends and settings to get my application to capture from v4l2 while using vaapi for decoding among a few other things.
Anyway it took me considerable time to realize that e.g. the ffmpeg backend doesn't support selecting the capture device by index.
I would like to have at least debug message in place to alert the user to as why capturing failed.
```c++
VideoCapture cap(0, CAP_FFMPEG);
```
The above line fails without a hint on why.

            cv2.imshow('Color Frame', frame)

import cv2

    out = cv2.cudacodec.VideoWriter('1_output_.mp4', (1920, 1080), 50)

title: How to contribute to OpenCV University?
body:
I have developed https://opencv.onweb.dev/ an easy to use, zero installation platform for experimenting with OpenCV and Python in the browser. It is based on WebAssembly and hence needs almost zero cloud infrastructure/resources to run. Another advantage is that it makes it possible to learn about and to experiment with OpenCV on platforms such as Chromebooks and on Android mobiles and tablets.
I would like to contribute this to OpenCV University, however, I cannot find the repository for the site...

title: CMake build on windows fails due to use of tar command
body:
OpenCV version 4.7.0
OS: Windows 10
Compiler: Microsoft (R) C/C++ Optimizing Compiler Version 19.34.31933 for x64
OpenCV cannot be configured with CMake on windows because the extraction of third party library ZIP files in `OpenCVDownload.cmake` uses the `cmake -E tar xzf` command.
This actually has been asked about on StackOverflow before: [link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61488479/cmake-error-problem-with-archive-write-finish-entry-cant-restore-time)
Indeed, this can be fixed on Windows if 7zip is installed by changing line 282 from
```
    execute_process(COMMAND ${CMAKE_COMMAND} -E tar xzf "${CACHE_CANDIDATE}"
                    WORKING_DIRECTORY "${DL_DESTINATION_DIR}"
                    RESULT_VARIABLE res)
```
to
```
    execute_process(COMMAND 7z x "${CACHE_CANDIDATE}"
                    WORKING_DIRECTORY "${DL_DESTINATION_DIR}"
                    RESULT_VARIABLE res)
```
There is an issue opened in the CMake repository about this: [link](https://gitlab.kitware.com/cmake/cmake/-/issues/19132)
And it appears that Paraview fixed this issue a year ago by changing the third party files to .tar.gz instead of .zip: [link](https://gitlab.kitware.com/paraview/paraview-superbuild/-/merge_requests/884)
So, could the third party files be hosted as .tar.gz instead of .zip in the case that that fixes the issue here?
This problem appears to come from a bug in [libarchive](https://github.com/libarchive/libarchive/issues/772) that was opened in 2016, so I doubt it is getting fixed any time soon.
With any zip file on Windows, using both PowerShell and cmd, this occurs
```
cmake -E tar xzf .\test.zip
CMake Error: Problem with archive_write_finish_entry(): Can't restore time
CMake Error: Problem extracting tar: .\test.zip
```

title: How to image process the on the original image like the of the given output.
OpenCV Java ... VideoCapture.open(URL) fails while Python3+cv2 is working on Win7.
Extract inliers from HL Image Stitcher
body:
The high level image stitcher provides a significantly accurate way to obtain a stitched image.
If you know the basics of it, in my testing it has never provided bad results.
If you want realtime stitching, you just resize the images to 2048x2048 and pyrDown 4 times till you get to 128x128.
Then initialize the stitcher with:
```
    static cv::Ptr<cv::Stitcher> Stitcher = cv::Stitcher::create(cv::Stitcher::SCANS);
    Stitcher->bundleAdjuster()->setConfThresh(10);
```
Over 5 years of experimentation, I have personally found this to be a highly robust method of finding the homography,
which is accessible through the C++ API using the rotations from the camera parameters.
Given that this class is so robust, and that I have found other methods of feature matching to produce way more outliers,
and that this class provides a status which can be used to determine if a frame is bad and should be dropped,
I think it would help people greatly if there was a way to extract the inliers found in the stitcher's estimation,
and preferably, a more generic method extracted from the original stitcher's code to get the original corners
which are inliers according to the stitcher, or no matching corners if the estimation failed.
I think the best way to implement this, would be to return a
```
    std::vector<std::vector<cv::Point2f>>
```
based on the amount of images provided.
The reason this would be an amazing feature to add, is that it would remove the need
to mess around with parameters given in the matching process, and it would significantly improve code simplicity.
This would allow for highly robust estimation of other types of poses, such as affine 3D using solvePnP.
I really hope you understand that this would be a feature worth your time to implement,
and I'm excited for it's potential.

title: pessimistic cv::RotatedRect::boundingRect()
body:
OpenCV 4.4.0
Even with integer center/even size, the boundingRect() estimation of a cv::RotatedRect() with a 0 angle may be one-pixel wider than expected.
This is not wrong per se (this is still a bounding rect), but it is a little pessimistic :
```
cv::Rect r(0, 0, 1280, 1024);
cv::RotatedRect rr(cv::Point2f(r.width/2.f, r.height/2.f),
                   cv::Size2f(1.f*r.width, 1.f*r.height),
                   0);
cv::Rect br = rr.boundingRect();
std::cout << "r:" << r << std::endl; //1280x1024
std::cout << "br:" << br << std::endl; //1281x1025
```
This may be normal since a RotatedRect is center-wise, so the size may have to be odd to match integer-pixel boundaries.
However, this is still pessimistic :
```
cv::Rect r(0, 0, 1281, 1025);
cv::RotatedRect rr(cv::Point2f(640, 512), cv::Size2f(1.f*r.width, 1.f*r.height), 0);
cv::Rect br = rr.boundingRect();
std::cout << "r:" << r << std::endl;//1281 x 1025
std::cout << "br:" << br << std::endl;//1283 x 1027
```

title: Mean computation of the same file differes between computers
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.1
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 16.4 Desktop / Server, latest  patches applied
- Compiler => gcc (Ubuntu 5.4.0-6ubuntu1~16.04.2) 5.4.0 20160609
Simple story: use **imread()** to load the data, then **split()** to divide it into the color planes. For each of them compute **mean()** value and print.
For my application I added several cucumber test cases, but found out that for JPEG files results are not consistent between different machines I clone github repo to.
Comp A: Lenovo G50 laptop with Ubuntu 16.4
Comp B: Dell 790 with Ubuntu Server 16.4
All the latest patches applied, the same procedure. wget file from the same source, run very simplistic code described above and exit.
Laptop - Lenovo G50
> wget http://www.fmwconcepts.com/misc_tests/canny/lena3.jpg
> repository/test$ ./test
>   105.4194
>    99.0473
>   180.2414
Dell 790
> wget http://www.fmwconcepts.com/misc_tests/canny/lena3.jpg
> repository/test$ ./test
>   105.4254
>    99.0470
>   180.2376
The difference is in R & B channels. Very small indeed but...
According to Matlab, ImageJ & even Photoshop (latest) histogram stats the good results are achieved on laptop, not on Dell 790.
For TIFF, PNG, PBM - no problems at all. Only for JPEG files there is difference in decoding.
As written above. No magic
```
void main() {
Mat img = imload('lena3.jpg');
std::vector<cv::Mat> planes;
split(img, planes);

for(auto&& p : planes)
 cout << endl << mean(p)
}
```

compile opencv with openMP
```cmake .. -DWITH_OPENMP=ON```
launch a program that uses parallel_for loop in OpenCV (i.e. samples/cpp/tutorial_code/core/parallel_backend/example-openmp.cpp), enable logs, and prioritize openMP if you have several parallel backed:

cd samples/cpp/tutorial_code/core/parallel_backend

export OPENCV_TRACE=1
export OPENCV_PARALLEL_PRIORITY_LIST=OPENMP,TBB
./opencv_example_openmp_backend
```

title: imgcodecs: gif: support IMREAD_UNCHANGE for 3ch data and IMREAD_GRAYSCALE
OpenCV Error: Gpu API call (invalid argument) in copy
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 2.4
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 16.04 LTS 64 bit
- Compiler => nvcc V8.0.61
Need to assign zeroes to a double GpuMat object using decimal form as integer form causes bug
**Code:**
```
cv::Mat a_h(977,1,DataType<double>::type);
gpu::GpuMat a_g(977,1,DataType<double>::type,0);
a_g.download(a_h);
cout << "a_h" << a_h;
```
gives an ```OpenCV Error: Gpu API call (invalid argument) in copy, file /.../opencv-2.4/modules/dynamicuda/include/opencv2/dynamicuda/dynamicuda.hpp, line 882```
**Workaround:**
However if I use 0.0 in ```gpu::GpuMat a_g(977,1,DataType<double>::type,0.0);``` the bug is solved!
(Compiled with ```nvcc `pkg-config --cflags opencv` filename.cu `pkg-config --libs opencv` -o main -arch=sm_50```)

227 228 229 230 231 232 233 234 235 236 237 238 239 240 241 242 243 244
245 246 247 248 249 250 251 252 253 254 255]
```python
_, thresh = cv2.threshold(gray, 200, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY)
print("thresh shape: {}, values: {}".format(thresh.shape, np.unique(thresh)))
cv2.imwrite(jpg_path, thresh)

title: OpenCV compilation as static library [Linux].
body:
I've compiled **OpenCV** as static library by downloading the source from **github**.
``` bash
 $ cmake WITH_GTK=ON WITH_GTK3=ON CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RELEASE BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF .
```
so I get all the libraries as static and try to compile my program according following Makefile:
``` makefile

CXX = g++
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11 

# Better sort the file order in relationship to `pkg-config --libs opencv` linking chain order.
OPENCV_A = libopencv_calib3d.a \  
    libopencv_contrib.a                    \
    libopencv_core.a                        \
    libopencv_features2d.a              \
    libopencv_flann.a                       \
    libopencv_gpu.a                         \
    libopencv_highgui.a                   \
    libopencv_imgproc.a                  \
    libopencv_legacy.a                    \
    libopencv_ml.a                          \
    libopencv_objdetect.a               \
    libopencv_ocl.a                         \
    libopencv_photo.a                    \
    libopencv_stitching.a                \
    libopencv_superres.a               \
    libopencv_ts.a                          \
    libopencv_video.a                    \
    libopencv_videostab.a             

default:
[TAB] $(CXX) $(CXXFLAGS) main.cpp $(OPENCV_A)
```
But it result in an error traceback: a linking problem (`dlsym linking` (**ld**) problem) related to the **OpenCV** dependencies (`libjpeg`, `libpng`, ...).
I find the following solution to but it doesn't take the way of a static linking:
``` bash
$ cmake WITH_GTK=ON WITH_GTK3=ON CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RELEASE BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF
$ make opencv_python2    
$ cd ./lib
$ sudo cp cv2.so /usr/local/lib
$ sudo ldconfig
$ sudo ln -s  /usr/local/lib/cv2.so /usr/local/lib/libcv2.so # It's the python2 shared libary.
$ cd /path/to/my/prg 
$ g++ -std=c++11 main.cpp -lcv2
```
So I compile a **C++** program with the **python2** `shared library`...
Is it correct or it risk to fail if I distribute my program on others system as mine ?
I do it like this because I don't want to pollute the **LIBPATH** either the **INCLUDEPATH** from the target host.

title: cv2.VideoCapture() not working
body:
- OpenCV => 4.1.0
- Operating System / Platform => Linux (Ubuntu 16.04)
- Language => Python 2.7.12
I've been working with a camera to adjust brightness and contrast using keyboard controls. When I used `cv2.VideoCapture(-1)`, the program worked perfectly. Since the goal is to use multiple cameras, I began using `cv2.VideoCapture(0)`. Without any other changes (only one camera plugged in to the USB bus, and found at `/dev/video0`), the program began to fail.
Looking closer at what caused the issue, I ended up with the following shortened code. If I tried to change two settings and take two pictures, the program would fail.  If I changed one setting, I could take as many pictures as I wanted without an error. Similarly, if I only took one picture, I could change the settings as many times as I wanted. However, whenever I tried to change a setting, it was always set to 0 until I next initialized my camera.
I would like to reiterate that `cv2.VideoCapture(-1)` was always successful. I also found that using `cv2.VideoCapture("/dev/video0")` worked. The issue was not the capabilities of my camera. However, `cv2.VideoCapture(0)` always failed. I tried adding a second camera, and found that `cv2.VideoCapture("/dev/video1")` worked and `cv2.VideoCapture(1)` failed.
##### Notes
cv2.CAP_PROP_BRIGHTNESS = 10
cv2.CAP_PROP_CONTRAST = 11
I tried placing a delay (`cv2.waitKey(500)`) between lines to no avail. I also tried this with only one USB camera plugged into the USB bus.
##### Code
import cv2
cam = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
r1 = cam.set(10,55)
ret0,img0 = cam.read()
r2 = cam.set(11,60)
ret1,img1 = cam.read()
r3 = cam.set(10,70)
print("Settings changed successfully?", r1,r2,r3)
print(cam.get(10), cam.get(11))
print("Pictures taken sucessfully?", ret0,ret1)
print("Cam open", cam.isOpened())
cam.open(0)
print("Reached end of program")
##### Output
libv4l2: error setting pixformat: Device or resource busy
('Settings changed successfully?', True, True, False)
(0.0, 0.0)
('Pictures taken sucessfully?', True, False)
('Cam open', False)
libv4l2: error setting pixformat: Device or resource busy
When I included `cam.open(0)`, the program would not continue to the next line of code.

title: Webcam not detected in opencv only.
body:
I have a program with opencv that worked well on my 32-bit windows, but when i updated to 64-bit, OpenCV is not detecting my webcam only in opencv programs. It is working every where.
OS - Windows 7 (64-bit)
Python version - 3.7
OpenCv version -   4.1.0.25
```
import cv2
import numpy as np
from pygame import mixer
import _thread

mixer.init()


def play_cords(file):
    mixer.music.load(file)
    mixer.music.play()




## Loop to test which number works but nothing worked
for i in range(0,501):
    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(i)

while cap.isOpened():
    _, img = cap.read(cap)
    img = cv2.resize(img,(300,300))

    hsv = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV)



    l_b = np.array([30,151,32])
    u_b = np.array([83,255,255])

    mask = cv2.inRange(hsv,l_b, u_b)

    res = cv2.bitwise_and(img,img,mask=mask)


    contours, _ = cv2.findContours(mask, cv2.RETR_TREE, cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)

    for c in contours:
        (x,y,w,h) = cv2.boundingRect(c)
        if cv2.contourArea(c) < 100:
            continue
        else:
            cv2.rectangle(img, (x,y), (x+w,y+h),(0,255,0),2)
            
            if x > 0 and y > 230  and x < 90 and y < 250  :                
                cv2.putText(img, 'A',(10,20),cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX, 1,(255,0,0),3)
                _thread.start_new_thread(play_cords,('Cords/A.mp3',))

            elif x > 91 and y > 230  and x < 180 and y < 250  :

            elif x > 181 and y > 230  and x < 300 and y < 250  :                
                cv2.putText(img, 'C',(10,20),cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX, 1,(255,0,0),3)
                _thread.start_new_thread(play_cords,('Cords/C.mp3',))

            

    cv2.drawContours(img, contours, -1, (0,0,255),2)

    img = cv2.line(img, (90,250),(90,400),(255,0,0),3)

    img = cv2.line(img, (180,250),(180,400),(255,0,0),3)

    k = cv2.waitKey(1)
    if k ==ord('q'):
        break
    if k ==27:
        break

    
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

Is the issue with my code or what? Please help because my assessment depends on this project.

title: Unable to execute cv2.imshow() the second time in Linux, works in Windows
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.9.0.80
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 22.04.4 LTS / Windows 11 pro
Python version: 3.11
I have another GUI script to open the Thread. When running the program in Linux, it has no issue when I first call cv2.imshow(), but after closing the cv2 window and reopen it, the script froze at cv2.imshow() (no error output). If I open a second thread while the first one is running, it will also freeze the first imshow().
None of these issues appears to happen when i run the scripts in Windows.
A few things I suspect:
The thread management are different in Linux?
imshow() does not close properly in Linux?
```python
class CaptureThread(threading.Thread):
    camera_index = None
    cap = None
    ret = None
    frame = None
    isRunning = False
    is_window_closed = False
    counter = 0
    timer = 0.0

    def request_stop(self):
        self.is_window_closed = True
        return

    def run(self):
        self.cap = cv2.VideoCapture(self.camera_index, cv2.CAP_V4L2)
        if global_vars.USE_CUSTOM_CAM_SETTINGS:
            self.cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS, global_vars.FPS)
            self.cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_WIDTH, global_vars.WIDTH)
            self.cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_HEIGHT, global_vars.HEIGHT)

        time.sleep(0.5)  # wait for camera to warm up
        print("Camera Index: ", self.camera_index)
        print("Opened Capture @ %s fps" % str(self.cap.get(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS)))
        while not self.is_window_closed and not global_vars.KILL_THREADS:
            self.ret, self.frame = self.cap.read()  # read the image from the camera
            self.isRunning = True
            if global_vars.DEBUG:
                self.counter = self.counter + 1
                if time.time() - self.timer >= 3:
                    print("Capture FPS: ", self.counter / (time.time() - self.timer))
                    self.counter = 0
                    self.timer = time.time()

class BodyThread(threading.Thread):
    stop_requested = False
    camera_index = None

    def __init__(self, camera_index):
        threading.Thread.__init__(self)
        # self.pcal = PoseCalculations()
        # self.capture = CaptureThread()
        self.camera_index = camera_index
        self.window_name = "Body Tracking " + str(camera_index)

    def request_stop(self):
        self.stop_requested = True

    def run(self):
        # pull frame
        capture = CaptureThread()
        capture.camera_index = self.camera_index
        capture.start()
        try:
            pcal = PoseCalculations()
            # create landmarker
            pose_landmarker = pose_landmarker_and_result()
            while capture.isRunning == False:
                print("Waiting for camera and capture thread.")
                time.sleep(0.5)
            print("Beginning capture")

            while capture.cap.isOpened() and not self.stop_requested:
                ti = time.time()
                ret = capture.ret
                image = capture.frame
                # mirror frame
                image = cv2.flip(image, 1)
                # update landmarker results
                result = pose_landmarker.detect_sync(image)
                # calculate fps
                tf = time.time()
                pose_fps = 1 / (tf - ti)

                # draw landmarks and stats on frame
                image = pcal.draw_landmarks(image, result)
                show_stats(image, pose_fps, pcal.left_elbow_angle, pcal.right_elbow_angle,
                           pcal.left_wrist_speed, pcal.right_wrist_speed)
                pcal.pose_recognition()
                if capture.camera_index == 2:
                    print("passed pcal.recognition")
                    # print(str(frame))
                # display live camera
                cv2.imshow(self.window_name, image)
                cv2.waitKey(1)
                if capture.camera_index == 2:
                    print("showing window running 2")
                if cv2.waitKey(1) == ord('q'):
                    self.request_stop()  # Request the current thread to stop
                    capture.request_stop()  # Request the CaptureThread to stop
                    break
                # Check if the window was closed
                if cv2.getWindowProperty(self.window_name, cv2.WND_PROP_VISIBLE) < 1:
                    self.request_stop()  # Request the current thread to stop
                    capture.request_stop()  # Request the CaptureThread to stop
                    capture.cap.release()
                    pose_landmarker.close()
                    cv2.destroyAllWindows()
                    print("destroyAllWindows X")
                    break  # Exit the loop
        finally:
            # release everything
            capture.cap.release()
            cv2.destroyAllWindows()
            print("destroyAllWindows while")
            pass
```

title: Changing the metric to calculate feature value!
body:
I am trying to modify the opencv traincascade source which is used to train the cascade classifier to detect objects. The original implementation of the Viola Jones algorithm uses only grayscale images to train the classifier. I am trying the change the metric used to calculate the haar feature value over an image. If anyone had already worked in improving or modifying this particular implementation in opencv please let me know where exactly should I make this modification for haar feature metric.
The original implementation calculates the feature value by taking the difference between sum of pixel intensities of the image under black portion of the window and the sum of pixel intensities under the white portion of the window. I am planning to modify it to taking the root mean square of all the differences in all the 3 channels.
I have done all the necessary changes in image reading storage and retrieval etc. Basically everything thing else to make it compliant except the feature value calculation. Please help me locate the function which calculates the feature value.
PS: The method also allows LBP and HOG features but I am interested only in modifying the Haar Features.

title: Explanation about stage thresholds in cascade training
Support for lowpass/anti-alias enabled resizing operations to support preprocessing operations for models trained with torchvision
body:
Torchvision and the python PIL library implement resizing operations that use a lowpass/anti-alias filter (see https://pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.Resize.html and https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/Image.html#PIL.Image.Image.resize). The "antialias" option is only supported for 'bilinear' and 'bicubic' interpolation. For torchvision those algorithms are the default with antialias enabled, meaning many computer vision models have been trained with these algorithms as a preprocessing step.
PIL and torchvision are very commonly used for computer vision models. Yet none of the interpolation options for opencv (https://docs.opencv.org/4.x/da/d54/group__imgproc__transform.html#ga5bb5a1fea74ea38e1a5445ca803ff121) for the resize operation in opencv get close to reproducing the result of those options. The difference for smaller images can be quite large, leading to large differences in model inference.
I would suggest that either:
* New InterpolationFlags are added for lowpass-enabled resizing
* Or an optional bool parameter lowpass or antialias is added to the resize method
Using a low pass filter separately gets closer, but not close enough to entirely reproduce the preprocessing of torchvision.
I'm not the only one having this issue:
* This issue has similar issues: https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/22134
* This blogpost has some errors, but does highlight the differences: https://zuru.tech/blog/the-dangers-behind-image-resizing
* I've discussed the same issue on the forum here, with a bit more detail: https://forum.opencv.org/t/anti-aliased-downscaling-in-opencv-for-ml-preprocessing-is-it-possible/17419

title: Implementing the auto correlation function
body:
I have often needed cross correlation/auto correlation function in openCV. [Matlab/Octave has a nice implementation called ``xcorr``.](https://se.mathworks.com/help/signal/ref/xcorr.html)
I know OpenCV has the ``matchTemplate`` function but it can not be used for calculating the autocorrelation of a function. ``matchTemplate`` expects a large image and a small(er) patch, and if the image and patch is the same size it will just return a number - not a function. I am also not sure a function called ``matchTemplate`` would be the best location for a potential autocorrelation function implementation.
I do have my own implementation of xcorr. Would it be possible to have it merged into opencv? I have read all your docs about contributing, but I am unsure of when/how you add new API's?

title: Wrong pixel value after resize with Linear interpolation
body:
OpenCV version: 4.7.0
Operating System / Platform: Debian 12
Compiler & compiler version: GCC 12.2.0
Pixel values are sometimes wrong (off by one) when using `cv2.resize` with `cv2.INTER_LINEAR_EXACT`.
To reproduce run the following script. It resizes 7x7 image to 3x3 image. The bottom right pixel value in the resized image is wrong, it should be 129, but is 128. It should be 129, because the bilinear interpolation for the pixel value is 128.555555555555, which should be rounded to 129. We can get the 128.555555555 by numeric derivation or by the `exact_computation` function in the following script.
```python
import cv2
import numpy as np
import math


def exact_computation(q11, q12, q21, q22, pix_x, pix_y, orig_x, orig_y, scale_x, scale_y):
    
    # 0.5 must be added, because pixel values are in the middle of a pixel
    x1 = math.floor((pix_x + 0.5) * scale_x) + 0.5
    y1 = math.floor((pix_y + 0.5) * scale_y) + 0.5

    x2 = math.ceil((pix_x + 0.5) * scale_x) + 0.5
    y2 = math.ceil((pix_y + 0.5) * scale_y) + 0.5

    x = (pix_x + 0.5) * scale_x
    y = (pix_y + 0.5) * scale_y

    # equations from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bilinear_interpolation section Repeated linear interpolation

    f_x_y1 = ((x2 - x) / (x2 - x1)) * q11 + ((x - x1) / (x2 - x1)) * q21
    f_x_y2 = ((x2 - x) / (x2 - x1)) * q12 + ((x - x1) / (x2 - x1)) * q22

    f_x_y = ((y2 - y) / (y2 - y1)) * f_x_y1 + ((y - y1) / (y2 - y1)) * f_x_y2

    return f_x_y


image = np.zeros((7, 7), dtype='uint8')

image[5, 5] = 78
image[5, 6] = 151
image[6, 5] = 245
image[6, 6] = 53

new_height = 3
new_width = 3

result = cv2.resize(image, (3, 3), cv2.INTER_LINEAR_EXACT)
print(result[2, 2])

correct_result = exact_computation(image[5, 5], image[6, 5], image[5, 6], image[6, 6], 2, 2, 7, 7, 7 / 3, 7 / 3)
print(correct_result)
assert result[2, 2] == 129
```

title: Extending Functions to Multidimensional Array Mat
body:
Many of the matrix operations/functions are only supported for matrix less than two dimensions. It would be nice to extend the functionality of these functions to include multidimensional arrays. This is something that MATLAB does which makes it an excellent software to work with when dealing with multidimensional data.
I will list a couple of functions here that do NOT have support for the multidimensional arrays but easily could be extended.
- findNonZero - Return N-dimensional points to where the nonzero values are located
- flip - Similar to MATLAB command where you can specify the dimension you want to flip.
As a additional note, from my extensive searching on multidimensional arrays, many people will do a vector list of slices instead of a 3D matrix because of the lack of support for 3D matrices. Hopefully, given enough time, I would like to tackle this issue and slowly start to add multidimensional functionality to functions.

title: masked images in MatchTemplate()
body:
// example for c++ user
OpenCV version: 4.6.0
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 20.04
Compiler & compiler version: GCC 9.4.0
// example for python user
OpenCV python version: 4.6.0.
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 20.04
Python version: 3.8.10
When we use mask in function MatchTemplate() with two the same size images we always have result 1x1 because in your function result is formed as img.size - templ.size + 1.
I siggest doing like this
```
int count = countNonZero(mask);
std::vector<Point> locations;
findNonZero(mask, locations);
    
Size corrSize(img.cols - (locations.at(count-1).x - locations.at(0).x + 1) + 1, 
    						img.rows - (locations.at(count-1).y - locations.at(0).y + 1) + 1);
```
Also I suggest rewrite some code of function in ```TM_CCOEFF``` / ```TM_CCOEFF_NORMED``` modes. For example,
```Mat templx_mask = mask.mul(mask.mul(templ - sum(mask.mul(templ)).div(mask_sum)));``` change to
```Mat templx_mask = mask.mul(templ - sum(mask.mul(templ)).div(mask_sum));```
Delete ```crossCorr(img, mask, img_mask_corr, Point(0, 0), 0, 0);``` because
```CCorr(I', T') = CCorr(I, T'*M) - sum(T'*M)*CCorr(I, M)/sum(M) = CCorr(I, T'*M)```.
Also I suggest not to do the same calculation in ```norm_templx``` and use ```norm(templx_mask, NORM_L2);```.

title: Is it possible to improve cv::cuda::remap performance by using Texture Mapping Units?
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
Graphics cards contain texture mapping units which are dedicated hardware for [remapping and interpolating textures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Texture_mapping_unit).
It can be seen in [cuda/filters.hpp](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/0052d46b8e33c7bfe0e1450e4bff28b88f455570/modules/core/include/opencv2/core/cuda/filters.hpp) which is used by [cuda/remap.cu](https://github.com/opencv/opencv_contrib/blob/ed1873bc2c58f1c2dc94f98c816be0d39068995f/modules/cudawarping/src/cuda/remap.cu) that OpenCV's CUDA remap is implemented in software without using the texture mapping unit.
It seems likely that the performance of `cv::cuda::remap` would be could be greatly improved if it used the dedicated hardware designed for texture mapping. Is there any technical reason why this can not be done?
CUDA appears to expose access to the texture mapping unit. [This presentation](https://on-demand.gputechconf.com/gtc-express/2011/presentations/texture_webinar_aug_2011.pdf) is a little old but explains the advantages well.

title: fitEllipse fails with a certain set of points
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.10.0
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 24.04.1 LTS
Python version: 3.12.3
cv2.fitEllipse function fails to fit an ellipse with a certain set of points. It seems that the returned angle of the ellipse should be around 90 degrees but 0 degree angle is returned instead.
I have added four examples: three sets of points for which ellipse fitting fails and one set of points for which ellipse is fitted successfully.
I am attaching the output of all four examples. No input is needed to reproduce the code. The points that need to be fitted are marked in red.
![elipse_fit_failed1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/efc41fd7-bc5f-474d-b374-d9b745b56cd0)
![elipse_fit_failed2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4281e918-967f-43ed-b88f-08fa790c1705)
![elipse_fit_failed3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ee27e1ae-1f2b-41d2-8a2a-a1da5a5ce628)
![elipse_fit_success](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/86b83c32-7535-466e-9424-aa8942bacdad)
This might be related to issue https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/26078
```python
import numpy as np
import cv2

# This is an example where ellipse fitting fails
points_to_fit = np.array([[37,111],[37,112],[36,113],[37,114],[37,115],[37,116],[37,117],[37,118],[36,119],[35,120],[34,120],[33,121],[32,121],[31,121],[30,122],[29,123],[28,123],[27,124],[26,124],[25,125],[25,126],[25,127],[25,128],[25,129],[254,121],[255,120],[255,119],[256,118],[256,117],[256,116],[256,115],[256,114],[256,113],[256,112],[256,111],[256,110],[256,109],[256,108],[256,107],[257,106],[257,105],[256,104],[257,103],[257,102],[257,101]])

# This is another example where ellipse fitting fails (subset of points in the first example). Uncomment the line below to check
#points_to_fit = np.array([[ 37, 105], [ 38, 106], [ 38, 107], [25,129], [254,121], [257, 103], [257, 102], [257, 101]])

# This is another example where ellipse fitting fails (subset of points in the second example). Uncomment the line below to check
# points_to_fit = np.array([[ 37, 105], [ 38, 106], [ 38, 107], [257, 103], [257, 102], [257, 101]])

# This is a different set of point where ellipse is fitted successfully. Uncomment the line below to check
# points_to_fit = np.array([[ 30, 105], [ 25, 110], [ 30, 115], [250, 105], [255, 110], [250, 115]]) 

e = cv2.fitEllipse(np.array(points_to_fit))

print(e)

img_tmp = np.ones((300, 300, 3), np.uint8) * 255

img_tmp = cv2.ellipse(img_tmp, e, (0,0,0), 1, cv2.LINE_AA)

for point in points_to_fit:
    img_tmp = cv2.circle(img_tmp, (int(round(point[0])), int(round(point[1]))), 1, (0,0,255), -1)


cv2.imshow("ellipse", img_tmp)
cv2.waitKey(0)  
cv2.destroyAllWindows()  
```

title: How to properly use opencl acceleration applied to 360 surround view
body:
360 surround view, multi-threaded processing of pictures from different cameras, opencv (4.5.5) for image correction and perspective transformation (remap), the CPU usage is very high, so use opencl (1.0) to accelerate, but with the increase of the number of threads, The processing time of a single thread increases several times, and the GPU occupancy rate is also high. How to optimize?
1. Use opencl 2.0?
2. Picture copy, umat has no data pointer, cannot use hardware RGA acceleration, can only use copyto, which is time-consuming
3. It feels that in multi-threading, the operation of opencl is blocked and queued, and the use of mutex has almost no effect on the time consumption

title: Cannot visualize mask created with cv2.inRange
body:
Hi,
I'm currently working with the gazebo camera, ROS and OpenCV. I want to process the camera images with this code but I cannot visualize in rqt_image_view the topic mask_pub. It doesn't return anything while I'm able to visualize the hsv_pub topic. Can anyone help me?
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
import sys
import cv2
import time
import numpy as np
from std_msgs.msg               import String
from sensor_msgs.msg            import Image
from cv_bridge                  import CvBridge, CvBridgeError
class MyClass:
def __init__(self):
# Subscriber nodes
self.bridge = CvBridge()
self.image_sub = rospy.Subscriber("/camera/image_raw", Image, self.camera_callback)
print ("<< Subscribed to topic /solo/solo_camera1/image_raw")
# Publisher nodes
print (">> Publishing image to topic image_blob")
self.image_pub = rospy.Publisher("/blob/img_blob", Image, queue_size=1)
self.mask_pub = rospy.Publisher("/bloom/img_mask",Image, queue_size=1)
self.hsv_pub = rospy.Publisher("/bloom/img_hsv",Image, queue_size=1)
def camera_callback(self,data):
try:
cv_image = self.bridge.imgmsg_to_cv2(data, desired_encoding="bgr8")
except CvBridgeError as e:
print(e)
blur = 10
h_min, h_max = 19, 31
s_min, s_max = 183, 240
v_min, v_max = 212, 255
hsv_min = (h_min, s_min, v_min)
hsv_max = (h_max, s_max, v_max)
cv_image2 = cv_image.copy()
cv_image2 = cv2.blur(cv_image2, (blur, blur))
hsv = cv2.cvtColor(cv_image2, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV)
mask = cv2.inRange(hsv, hsv_min, hsv_max, None)
mask2 = mask.copy()
mask2 = cv2.dilate(mask2, None)
mask2 = cv2.erode(mask2, None)
reversemask = 255-mask2
mask2 = mask.copy()
imgCanny = cv2.Canny(mask2, 50, 50)
contours, hierarchy = cv2.findContours(imgCanny, cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL, cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_NONE)
try:
self.image_pub.publish(self.bridge.cv2_to_imgmsg(cv_image, "bgr8"))
self.hsv_pub.publish(self.bridge.cv2_to_imgmsg(hsv, "bgr8"))
self.mask_pub.publish(self.bridge.cv2_to_imgmsg(reversemask,  "8UC1"))
except CvBridgeError as e:
print(e)
def main():
rospy.init_node('my_node', anonymous=True)
rospy.loginfo('image_pub node started')
trial = MyClass()
try:
rospy.spin()
except KeyboardInterrupt:
print("Shutting Down")
if __name__ == '__main__':
main()

body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
stitching_detailed.py: It can not be spliced well , Request help
```
Direction transverse

1.jpg
4500 * 16000

2.jpg
4500 * 16000

3.jpg
4500 * 16000
```
I need to splice, but I can't complete the requirements very well. I need help
Pictures can be sent to email

title: OpenCV build - cmake don't recognize GTK2 or GTK3
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.7.0
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 20.04
- Compiler => g++ (Ubuntu 9.4.0-1ubuntu1~20.04.1) 9.4.0
- C++ Standard: 11
I am trying to create OpenCV with the GTK GUI. I have `GTK2` and `GTK3` installed on the system, but neither version is recognised by cmake.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/62354721/230026534-66053f4e-67d3-45cd-93c2-4902365400c7.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/62354721/230026727-07d64f8c-c453-4048-9d23-3608b1c94663.png)
The output after executing cmake remains
**GUI: NONE**
**GTK+:NO**
I execute the cmake command as follows:
`cmake -DPYTHON_DEFAULT_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) -DWITH_GTK=ON ../opencv`
or
`cmake -DPYTHON_DEFAULT_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) -DWITH_GTK=ON -DWITH_GTK_2_X=ON ../opencv`
git clone https://github.com/opencv/opencv.git
mkdir -p build && cd build
cmake ../opencv (default, custom look above)
Thank you for your help
