title: The dnn Tensorflow parser doesn't support ExpandDims layer with the new dnn engine
body:
OpenCV 5.x
OpenCV dnn uses nchw layout, Tensorflow uses nhwc layout. When dnn parser handles a tensorflow layer with an axis argument, it should convert the axis from nhwc to ncwh. However the parser doesn't know the number of dimensions of the input tensor, so it runs the shape inference of all previous layers to calculate it. But the new dnn engine doesn't support shape inference when the network is not fully built.
run Test_TensorFlow_layers.ExpandDims tests

title: randn and randu unexpected behavior using >1 channel images
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.1.1
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 18.04
- Compiler => Python 3.6
Documentation for `cv2.randn` says  _Fills the array with normally distributed random numbers_, so I expect it to fill whole array, but it actually works only with the channel 0. Later it says _dst - output array of random numbers; the array must be pre-allocated and have 1 to 4 channels_, means 3 channel images are allowed and it should fill the whole array. The same for `cv2.randu`.
This code
```.py
noise = np.zeros((10,10,3), dtype=np.uint8)
print(cv2.randn(noise, 128, 50)[:,:,1:])
print(cv2.randu(noise, 0, 255)[:,:,1:])
```
prints only zeros.

title: Create _InputArray/_OutputArray from fixed size array
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
I think we could add an extra constructor to `_InputArray`/`_OutputArray` so that it can convert directly from a C++ fixed size array, like this:
```c++
template<typename _Tp, std::size_t _Nm> _InputArray(const _Tp (&vec)[_Nm]);
```
Traditionally, we need to explicitly convert it to a combination of pointer + size, like:
```c++
auto arr = _InputArray(vec, std::size(vec));
```
It would be more convenient to have something like:
```c++
auto arr = _InputArray(vec);
```

OpenCv erosion-function affecting not only edges
body:
I run the cv2.erode function on the left image below, and get the right image as a result. As expected, the edges of the object in the right image has been eroded compared to the left. But what is NOT expected is that areas within the object - far from the edges - also is affected. The most obvious part is the dark vertical area in the bottom left in the image. As seen, this dark area has grown bigger in the right image, even though erosion is only supposed to affect the edges of the object. Why is this happening?
```
kernel_size = 3
kernel = np.ones((kernel_size, kernel_size), np.uint8)
im_eroded = cv2.erode(im, kernel, iterations=1)

libjpeg-turbo faster execution, slower VideoCapture() framerate?
body:
Operating System: Lubuntu 18.04
Platform: x86_64
OpenCV Version: 4.1.0
CPU: Intel Atom Z8350
Memory: 2 GB
Compile Option In Question: BUILD_JPEG = YES/NO
Example code (python):
cap = cv.VideoCapture()
# Set prop
cap.open(0, apiPreference=cv.CAP_V4L2)
cap.set(cv.CAP_PROP_FOURCC, cv.VideoWriter_fourcc('M', 'J', 'P', 'G'))
cap.set(cv.CAP_PROP_FRAME_WIDTH, 1280)
cap.set(cv.CAP_PROP_FRAME_HEIGHT, 960)
cap.set(cv.CAP_PROP_FPS, 30.0)
cap.set(cv.CAP_PROP_AUTO_EXPOSURE, True)
if not cap.isOpened():
logger.error("Could not open camera.")
exit(-1)
while True:
# Capture frame
ret, frame = cap.read()
if not ret:
logger.warn("Could not receive frame.")
cv.imshow('webcam', frame)
if cv.waitKey(1) == ord('q'):
break
When compiling with BUILD_JPEG enabled (thus building libjpeg_turbo in the 3rdparty directory), using the above code I am only able to achieve 17 FPS when 30 is requested, at ~30% CPU utilization. This utilization is restricted to a single core.
However, when compiling using the system libjpeg library, the same code yields a maximum capture FPS of >= 30 FPS, when 30 is requested. However, the CPU utilization is considerably higher at 50-60%. The utilization is spread evenly over 3-4 cores.
I would prefer the higher framerate over less CPU overhead, but what I do not understand is why using libjpeg-turbo produces a lower framerate.
Is there something about libjpeg-turbo that requires it to operate on a single thread? How can I enable multiple threads?

title: Confusing versions of StereoMatcher in v4.2 documentation
body:
I'm browsing OpenCV 4.2 documentation, which shows two versions of `StereoMatcher` class:
1. `cv::StereoMatcher` declared in `opencv2/calib3d.hpp`
2. `cv::stereo::StereoMatcher` declared in `opencv2/stereo.hpp`

title: Creating a mask from WKT multipolygons using openCV and tiff libraries?
body:
I'm trying to create a binary mask from a WKT multipolygon which is a csv file with python openCV, shapely and tiff libraries in Jupiter Notebook. There's also another csv file that contains Xmax and Ymin of the image. However, I keep getting a black image. here's the code:
```
from collections import defaultdict
import csv, sys, cv2
from shapely.geometry import MultiPolygon, Polygon
import shapely.wkt
import shapely.affinity
import numpy as np
import tifffile as tiff
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

maxInt = sys.maxsize
decrement = True


while decrement:
    # decrease the maxInt value by factor 10 
    # as long as the OverflowError occurs.

    decrement = False
    try:
        csv.field_size_limit(maxInt)
    except OverflowError:
        maxInt = int(maxInt/10)
        decrement = True


x_max = y_min = None
for _im_id, _x, _y in csv.reader(open('GridSize.csv')):
    if _im_id == IM_ID:
        x_max, y_min = float(_x), float(_y)
        break

sample_polygons = None
for _im_id, _poly_type, _poly in csv.reader(open('WKT_polygons.csv')):
    if _im_id == IM_ID and _poly_type == POLY_TYPE:
        sample_polygons = shapely.wkt.loads(_poly)
        break


im_rgb = tiff.imread('image.tif')
im_size = im_rgb.shape[:2]

def polygons_mask(polygons):
    img_mask = np.zeros(im_size, np.uint8)
    if not polygons:
        return img_mask
    int_coords = lambda x: np.array(x).round().astype(np.int32)
    exteriors = [int_coords(poly.exterior.coords) for poly in polygons]
    interiors = [int_coords(pi.coords) for poly in polygons
                 for pi in poly.interiors]
    cv2.fillPoly(img_mask, exteriors, 1)
    cv2.fillPoly(img_mask, interiors, 0)
    return img_mask

_mask = polygons_mask(sample_polygons)
tiff.imshow(_mask)[0]

```
Any ideas?
![empty mask](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14327463/27718063-e73f5e36-5d7b-11e7-88ff-f854c3f6062e.png)

title: */highgui/src/window.cpp:597: error: (-213:The function/feature is not implemented) The library is compiled without QT support in function 'createButton'. Any OpenCV version. Need feature implemented
Implementation: CUDA support in the pre-built binaries of OpenCV
body:
Dear OpenCV Development Team,
I am upset and have much **dissatisfaction** regarding the absence of CUDA support in the pre-built binaries of OpenCV available through pip.
As a user who relies heavily on GPU acceleration for computational tasks, the lack of CUDA support in the standard opencv-python and opencv-python-headless packages is a significant limitation. CUDA support is crucial for optimizing performance in many applications, especially in the field of computer vision and deep learning.
While I understand that CUDA is an optional feature and building OpenCV with CUDA support from source is possible, this process is complex and time-consuming. Many users, including myself, would greatly benefit from having CUDA support available in the pre-built binaries to streamline the installation process and leverage GPU acceleration efficiently.
I kindly request that the OpenCV team consider providing pre-built binaries with CUDA support included by default. This enhancement would significantly improve the accessibility and performance of OpenCV for users who rely on GPU acceleration for their projects.
Proposed Solution:
Build System Integration:
Update Build Scripts: Modify the build scripts used for creating OpenCV binaries to include options for CUDA support. This involves configuring CMake to enable CUDA-related features during the build process.
Use Continuous Integration (CI): Incorporate CUDA support into the CI pipeline to ensure that CUDA-enabled binaries are built and tested regularly. Platforms like GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or Jenkins can be configured to handle CUDA builds.
Binary Distribution:
Pre-built Binaries: Provide CUDA-enabled binaries through pip. This could be achieved by creating separate packages or adding CUDA support as an optional feature within the existing package.
Package Naming Convention: Use a clear naming convention for CUDA-enabled packages, such as opencv-python-cuda, to differentiate them from the standard binaries.
Documentation and Support:
Update Documentation: Clearly document the availability of CUDA-enabled binaries, including installation instructions, compatibility details, and usage guidelines.
Provide Support: Offer support channels or forums where users can seek assistance related to CUDA support in OpenCV. This can help address any issues or questions that arise.
Community and User Feedback:
Gather Feedback: Engage with the OpenCV user community to gather feedback on the CUDA support implementation. This can be done through surveys, forums, or GitHub issues.
Iterate Based on Feedback: Continuously improve the CUDA support based on user feedback and performance metrics.
By adopting these measures, OpenCV can provide a more robust solution for users who require GPU acceleration, thereby enhancing the overall user experience and broadening the applicability of OpenCV in high-performance computing scenarios.
Thank you for considering this proposal. I look forward to your feedback and the possibility of seeing CUDA support included in future OpenCV releases.

I had built OpenCV from source with CUDA acceleration, and copied the .pyd into my Anaconda environment. However, I could not do any submodule imports, and I cannot place ```import cv2``` on anywhere other than the first line.
```
import cv2 #this is the only way to import without throwing any error.

import cv2.dnn # does not work

import library1
import library2
...
import cv2 # doesn't work either

 ```
Failing to do so would produce the following error.
```
ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified procedure could not be found.
```
Is this a build issue? A dependency issue? Or an environment issue? Is this problem serious enough to affect the library's runtime performance?

title: OpenCV 4.7 cuda 12.0 VS 22 lnk2019 error
imread and imwrite do not exist in OpenCV Android SDK
body:
I'm trying to compile some code that uses the Android OpenCV SDK (version 4.1.1).
One of the files cannot be compiled, because of an undefined reference :
```
openalpr/src/misc_utilities/prepcharsfortraining.cpp:224: error: undefined reference to 'cv::imwrite(cv::String const&, cv::_InputArray const&, std::__ndk1::vector<int, std::__ndk1::allocator<int> > const&)'
```
Excerpt of the incriminating file :
```
#include "opencv2/highgui/highgui.hpp"
#include "opencv2/imgproc/imgproc.hpp"

#include <iostream>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <fstream>
#include <sys/stat.h>
#include "support/filesystem.h"
#include "../tclap/CmdLine.h"
#include "support/utf8.h"

using namespace std;
using namespace cv;
using namespace alpr;

int main(void){
   ...
   imwrite("combined.tif", bigTif);
}
```
`CMakeLists.txt` contains the following statements (among others)
```
# Discover OpenCV directory automatically
find_path(OpenCV_DIR
    NAMES OpenCVConfig.cmake
    HINTS ${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/../libraries/opencv/
    /storage/projects/alpr/libraries/opencv/
    )

# Opencv Package
FIND_PACKAGE( OpenCV REQUIRED )

#Some code omitted for clarity ...

 TARGET_LINK_LIBRARIES(alpr
    ${OPENALPR_LIB}
    ${STATE_DETECTION_LIB}
    support
    video
    ${OpenCV_LIBS}
    ${Tesseract_LIBRARIES}
    ${Extra_LIBS}
  )
```
I'm using the following cmake intruction :
```
cmake \
    -DANDROID_TOOLCHAIN=clang\
    -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=$NDK_ROOT/build/cmake/android.toolchain.cmake \
    -DANDROID_NDK=$NDK_ROOT \
    -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
    -DANDROID_PLATFORM=$ANDROID_PLATFORM \
    -DANDROID_ABI="$i" \
    -DANDROID_STL=c++_shared \
    -DANDROID_CPP_FEATURES="rtti exceptions" \
    -DTesseract_INCLUDE_BASEAPI_DIR=$TESSERACT_SRC_DIR/api \
    -DTesseract_INCLUDE_CCSTRUCT_DIR=$TESSERACT_SRC_DIR/ccstruct \
    -DTesseract_INCLUDE_CCMAIN_DIR=$TESSERACT_SRC_DIR/ccmain \
    -DTesseract_INCLUDE_CCUTIL_DIR=$TESSERACT_SRC_DIR/ccutil \
    -DTesseract_LIB=$TESSERACT_LIB_DIR/libtess.so \
    -DLeptonica_LIB=$TESSERACT_LIB_DIR/liblept.so \
    -DOpenCV_DIR=$SCRIPTPATH/../OpenCV-android-sdk/sdk/native/jni \
    -DJAVA_AWT_LIBRARY=$JAVA_AWT_LIBRARY \
    -DJAVA_JVM_LIBRARY=$JAVA_JVM_LIBRARY \
    -DJAVA_INCLUDE_PATH=$JAVA_INCLUDE_PATH \
    -DJAVA_INCLUDE_PATH2=$JAVA_INCLUDE_PATH2 \
    -DJAVA_AWT_INCLUDE_PATH=$JAVA_AWT_INCLUDE_PATH \
    -DPngt_LIB=$TESSERACT_LIB_DIR/libpngt.so \
    -DJpgt_LIB=$TESSERACT_LIB_DIR/libjpgt.so \
    -DJnigraphics_LIB=$NDK_ROOT/platforms/$ANDROID_PLATFORM/arch-$arch/usr/$lib/libjnigraphics.so \
    -DANDROID_ARM_MODE=arm \
    ../../src/

```
I can't understand why imread and imwrite cannot be referenced .. Is it missing from the Android SDK ? Tried NDK 20, 19, 16 and 14 with no luck

title: meanStdDev bug while RUN_TESTS for opencv_test_core
body:
OpenCV version: 4.6.0 with contrib module
Operating System / Platform: Windows 10 educational
Compiler & compiler version: Visual Studio 2022
When I run the RUN_TESTS for the compiled opencv 4.6 (with contrib module 4.6), and without the "with_IPP" and "with_OPENCL" options, the RUN_TESTS will give me such issue:
> [----------] 1 test from Core_MeanStdDev/ElemWiseTest
> [ RUN      ] Core_MeanStdDev/ElemWiseTest.accuracy/0, where GetParam() = 16-byte object <10-63 C9-AB 98-01 00-00 30-46 E1-AB 98-01 00-00>
> E:\MyDrivers\api\opencv-4.6.0\sources\modules\core\test\test_arithm.cpp(1505): error: too big relative difference (0.31746958239660777 > 0.12431653146558173) between 64fC4 2-dim (1 x 2) 'dst0' and 'dst' at [0, 7].
> - dst0:
> 84.96203571109626, 292.2867051351412, 117.2226766848602, 145.7930433566753, 604.0160556371524, 573.5476616810923, 566.4748429192583, *427.6311850711165*.
> - dst:
> 84.96203571109626, 292.2867051351412, 117.2226766848602, 145.7930433566753, 604.0160556371524, 573.5476616810923, 598.5923379483519, *627.0030516093238*.
> src[0] ~ 64fC4 2-dim (1 x 18)
> testCase #343
Actually, I tried to print the tested src Mat and perform the calculation myself, the dst0 is correct (for the 3rd column with conv=566.47), while the dst is wrong. The src Mat (4 channels) is as follows:
> -73.62713441    280.4789855 -334.6340674    -219.7272904
> -607.7548047    -755.2156259    -997.4985087    268.3496785
> 149.2775288 576.5006519 498.2970279 267.1693062
> -874.7861883    396.2149921 505.3383681 -8.805473687
> 165.0991975 -987.3847146    657.0005484 538.2661919
> 753.5044804 685.4099242 425.0569732 -0.026177985
> -772.25543  -21.399704  -104.2522104    391.1741008
> -225.6105867    852.20889   479.8527143 480.3463748
> 845.4971929 705.4109547 360.1990013 -283.3870267
> 941.9562699 552.0246371 39.55008381 33.0637321
> -184.7803476    362.3189292 -644.0262049    924.8139461
> 647.9731311 897.8831332 579.705476  266.3566914
> 815.6293445 561.8476381 986.5666662 399.2300557
> 313.350129  676.4778877 854.5349978 -585.6877577
> 615.7872755 -161.5565781    -263.7773417    85.10474015
> 342.8826109 -720.2919446    31.15492855 926.3618211
> -886.1881474    968.2679416 -950.228709 -609.7302309
> -436.6378786    391.9646944 -12.83156304    -248.5979009
Also I found that in modules\core\src\mean.dispatch.cpp, after Line 593, when I print out sq as
>     double scale = nz0 ? 1./nz0 : 0.;
>     std::cout << sq[0] << ' ' << sq[1] << ' ' << sq[2] << ' ' << sq[3] << std::endl;
>     for( k = 0; k < cn; k++ )
>         s[k] *= scale;
>         sq[k] = std::sqrt(std::max(sq[k]*scale - s[k]*s[k], 0.));
I got: 6.69697e+06 7.45899e+06 6.69697e+06 7.45899e+06
The 1st and 2nd numbers are correct, but the 3rd and 4th number of sq are just the same as the 1st and 2nd. That is not possible for the given randomly generated src Mat.
I guess the problem comes from the SumSqrFunc? Or why should the sq results wrong?
### Update:
More information: the tested src is 64FC4, I don't know whether it is the key point.
However, I found a solution:
Interestingly, I found that the problem lies here (core\src\mean_simd.hpp):
![198824972-822c5bb9-c4be-440f-9386-d03bd49bb2b6](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16728292/198825279-f9af2bb9-024b-4248-b722-0038ad437f64.png)
When I add v2 and v3 variables here, everything works fine:
![198824995-836703b5-f9f7-4d20-a50f-39475843e043](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16728292/198825285-8cf44890-aff1-40fe-bce4-247fbab6fa88.png)
I think there is something wrong with the optimization (IPP? OVX? I have shut with_IPP down already...) of this function, and somehow the v0, v1 values in core\src\mean_simd.hpp line 227 have not been changed, as I mentioned before, the sqsum values of 2 and 3 are the same as 0 and 1 in such case, and when I change v0 v1 to v2 v3 (new variables), everything is OK.
Illustrated above.

title: Error when Stitching Images with >10.000 Pixels
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.2.0
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 64 Bit
- Compiler => Spyder 4.0.1 with Python 3.7.3
The goal is to stitch high resolution scans/images with >10,000 x 10,000 pixel together. When trying to stitch them it immediately returns status 1 ("no success!") without even trying to stitch the images. It's surely not due to a missing overlap or so since if the Images are rescaled to a percentage of the size it works.
Example data can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/open?id=11Mb_RBrjG9HpbL6Zsgd401mQ7QTbXHS9).
It contains in each case two high resolution images cropped into two subframes. The Bathy subframes represent images >10.000 px and the Historic subframes < 10.000 px:
1. [Bathy](https://eoimages.gsfc.nasa.gov/images/imagerecords/73000/73726/world.topo.bathy.200406.3x21600x10800.jpg)
into 14133 px x 10783 px and 12191 px x 10775 px
2. [Historic](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pizigani_1367_Chart_10MB.jpg)
into 6641 px x 6081 px and 5433 px x 6025 px
I use the following code:
```python
import os
import cv2

images = r"path_to_folder/Stitching/Bathy"
os.chdir(images)

images = []
for image in os.listdir():
    image = cv2.imread(image)
    print('Image Dimensions : ',image.shape)
    images.append(image)

stitcher = cv2.Stitcher_create()
status, pano  = stitcher.stitch(images)

if status == 0:
    cv2.imshow("Pano", pano)
    cv2.waitKey(0)
    cv2.destroyAllWindows()
else:
    print("no success!")
```
The historic images (<10.000 px subframes) are loaded, it takes a short time to run and the stitched image is shown. The bathymetric images (>10.000 px subframes) are loaded, too, but then it immediately returns status 1 ("no success!") without even trying to stitch the images. If the Images are rescaled to e.g. 10% of the size it works:
```python
for image in os.listdir():
    image = cv2.imread(image)
    print('Image Dimensions : ',image.shape)
    scale_percent = 10 
    width = int(image.shape[1] * scale_percent / 100)
    height = int(image.shape[0] * scale_percent / 100)
    dim = (width, height)
    image = cv2.resize(image, dim, interpolation = cv2.INTER_AREA) 
    print('Resized Dimensions : ',image.shape) 
    images.append(image)
```
answers.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found solution

title: Support GraphCutSeamFinder in stitching_detailed.py
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
with the release of [OpenCV 4.7](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/releases/tag/4.7.0) this [PR](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/pull/22329) becomes available so we can add
```
SEAM_FIND_CHOICES['gc_color'] = cv.detail_GraphCutSeamFinder('COST_COLOR')
SEAM_FIND_CHOICES['gc_colorgrad'] = cv.detail_GraphCutSeamFinder('COST_COLOR_GRAD')
```
to https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/4.x/samples/python/stitching_detailed.py
Previously it was [removed](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/commit/dbd65a3b01bbeca7405bf4c5c3971c85f5fa6d89) since python bindings were corrupt. Thanks to @chinery for fixing this.

title: I can't connect ip camera to opencv (-215:Assertion failed)
body:
##### System information (version)
Hi, there! How are you?
Well, I'm using my cell phone as a camera with droidcam and i'm trying to connect a ip camera into opencv, but I'm reciving this message error everytime I try:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):

    cv2.imshow('IPWebcam',img)
cv2.error: OpenCV(4.2.0) C:\projects\opencv-python\opencv\modules\highgui\src\window.cpp:376: error: (-215:Assertion failed) size.width>0 && size.height>0 in function 'cv::imshow'
```
Here's my full code:
```
import urllib.request
import cv2
import numpy as np
import time
URL = "http://xxx.xxx.x.xxx:xxxx/"
while True:
    img_arr = np.array(bytearray(urllib.request.urlopen(URL).read()),dtype=np.uint8)
    img = cv2.imdecode(img_arr,-1)
    #imS = cv2.resize(img,(960, 540))
    cv2.imshow('IPWebcam',img)

title: How to build for FreeBSD 13.1 ?
body:
System information (version):
- OpenCV => 4.6.0
- Operating System / Platform => FreeBSD 13.1
- Compiler => clang/llvm and gcc 11
-KDE5 plasma
Has anyone able to compile OpenCV 4.6.0 successfully for FreeBSD 13.1?
If so what was used to compile opencv and also what commands was used?

title: DISOptical flow result bad
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => : 4.1.0.25
- Operating System / Platform => :Unbuntu16.04 64 Bit
- Compiler => :Pycharm Community Edition2017.2
I use Opencv 4.1.0.25 for DIS Optical Flow, code as below , but I got the result  of UseMeanNormalization to calc distances of current imgs and reference image, I got results parameter "flows" all zeros. This could not be true result, for I picture the moving object. I don't know why this happened. And I try on OpenCV Online Document for help, but I could not find detail descriptions on how to use python code of DIS Optical flow
```.python
    ref = np.uint8(allimgs[0] * 255)
    motions = []
    flows = []
    h, w = ref.shape
    count = h * w
    totalMotions = 0
    #
    dis = cv2.DISOpticalFlow_create(1) #for use PRESET_FAST = 1
    dis.setUseMeanNormalization(True)
    flows.append(np.zeros((h, w), np.float32))
    for i in range(1, len(allimgs)):
        curr_img = np.uint8(allimgs[i] * 255)
        flow = np.zeros((h, w), np.float32)
        dis.calc(ref, curr_img, flow)
        flows.append(flow)
    ```

title: calibrateCameraRO using iFixedPoint: meaning and odd behavior
body:
according to the documentation the iFixedPoint in the calibrateCameraRO acts as a switch for calibration method selection beside to be  the index of the 3D object point to be fixed.
Here the documentation lines:
iFixedPoint: The index of the 3D object point in objectPoints[0] to be fixed. It also acts as a switch for calibration method selection. If object-releasing method to be used, pass in the parameter in the range of [1, objectPoints[0].size()-2], otherwise a value out of this range will make standard calibration method selected. Usually the top-right corner point of the calibration board grid is recommended to be fixed when object-releasing method being utilized. According to [219], two other points are also fixed. In this implementation, objectPoints[0].front and objectPoints[0].back.z are used. With object-releasing method, accurate rvecs, tvecs and newObjPoints are only possible if coordinates of these three fixed points are accurate enough.
Reading that what I have understood is that if I put a number out of the range [1, objectPoints[0].size()-2] the old calibration code will be run. I check this and it is true.
When I try to put as fixedPoint the index 1 a the other calibration is running (slower but it is ok) that give me different results. However I try all the other index in the range and the slower algorithm is running but it give me the same result of running the code with iFixedPoint=-1
What I missing?

rmats = cv.detail.waveCorrect(rmats, wave_correct)
for idx, cam in enumerate(cameras):
cam.R = rmats[idx]
corners = []
masks_warped = []
images_warped = []
sizes = []
masks = []
for i in range(0, num_images):
um = cv.UMat(255 * np.ones((images[i].shape[0], images[i].shape[1]), np.uint8))
masks.append(um)
warper = cv.PyRotationWarper(warp_type, warped_image_scale * seam_work_aspect)  # warper could be nullptr?
for idx in range(0, num_images):
K = cameras[idx].K().astype(np.float32)
swa = seam_work_aspect
K[0, 0] *= swa
K[0, 2] *= swa
K[1, 1] *= swa
K[1, 2] *= swa
corner, image_wp = warper.warp(images[idx], K, cameras[idx].R, cv.INTER_LINEAR, cv.BORDER_REFLECT)
corners.append(corner)
sizes.append((image_wp.shape[1], image_wp.shape[0]))
images_warped.append(image_wp)
p, mask_wp = warper.warp(masks[idx], K, cameras[idx].R, cv.INTER_NEAREST, cv.BORDER_CONSTANT)
masks_warped.append(mask_wp.get())
images_warped_f = []
for img in images_warped:
imgf = img.astype(np.float32)
images_warped_f.append(imgf)
compensator = get_compensator(args)
compensator.feed(corners=corners, images=images_warped, masks=masks_warped)
seam_finder = SEAM_FIND_CHOICES[args.seam]
masks_warped = seam_finder.find(images_warped_f, corners, masks_warped)
compose_scale = 1
corners = []
sizes = []
blender = None
timelapser = None
# https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/4.x/samples/cpp/stitching_detailed.cpp#L725 ?
for idx, name in enumerate(img_names):
full_img = cv.imread(name)
if not is_compose_scale_set:
if compose_megapix > 0:
compose_scale = min(1.0, np.sqrt(compose_megapix * 1e6 / (full_img.shape[0] * full_img.shape[1])))
is_compose_scale_set = True
compose_work_aspect = compose_scale / work_scale
warped_image_scale *= compose_work_aspect
warper = cv.PyRotationWarper(warp_type, warped_image_scale)
for i in range(0, len(img_names)):
cameras[i].focal *= compose_work_aspect
cameras[i].ppx *= compose_work_aspect
cameras[i].ppy *= compose_work_aspect
sz = (int(round(full_img_sizes[i][0] * compose_scale)),
int(round(full_img_sizes[i][1] * compose_scale)))
K = cameras[i].K().astype(np.float32)
roi = warper.warpRoi(sz, K, cameras[i].R)
corners.append(roi[0:2])
sizes.append(roi[2:4])
if abs(compose_scale - 1) > 1e-1:
img = cv.resize(src=full_img, dsize=None, fx=compose_scale, fy=compose_scale,
interpolation=cv.INTER_LINEAR_EXACT)
else:
img = full_img
_img_size = (img.shape[1], img.shape[0])
K = cameras[idx].K().astype(np.float32)
corner, image_warped = warper.warp(img, K, cameras[idx].R, cv.INTER_LINEAR, cv.BORDER_REFLECT)
mask = 255 * np.ones((img.shape[0], img.shape[1]), np.uint8)
p, mask_warped = warper.warp(mask, K, cameras[idx].R, cv.INTER_NEAREST, cv.BORDER_CONSTANT)
compensator.apply(idx, corners[idx], image_warped, mask_warped)
image_warped_s = image_warped.astype(np.int16)
dilated_mask = cv.dilate(masks_warped[idx], None)
seam_mask = cv.resize(dilated_mask, (mask_warped.shape[1], mask_warped.shape[0]), 0, 0, cv.INTER_LINEAR_EXACT)
mask_warped = cv.bitwise_and(seam_mask, mask_warped)
if blender is None and not timelapse:
blender = cv.detail.Blender_createDefault(cv.detail.Blender_NO)
dst_sz = cv.detail.resultRoi(corners=corners, sizes=sizes)
blend_width = np.sqrt(dst_sz[2] * dst_sz[3]) * blend_strength / 100
if blend_width < 1:
blender = cv.detail.Blender_createDefault(cv.detail.Blender_NO)
elif blend_type == "multiband":
blender = cv.detail_MultiBandBlender()
blender.setNumBands((np.log(blend_width) / np.log(2.) - 1.).astype(np.int32))
elif blend_type == "feather":
blender = cv.detail_FeatherBlender()
blender.setSharpness(1. / blend_width)
blender.prepare(dst_sz)
elif timelapser is None and timelapse:
timelapser = cv.detail.Timelapser_createDefault(timelapse_type)
timelapser.initialize(corners, sizes)
if timelapse:
ma_tones = np.ones((image_warped_s.shape[0], image_warped_s.shape[1]), np.uint8)
timelapser.process(image_warped_s, ma_tones, corners[idx])
pos_s = img_names[idx].rfind("/")
if pos_s == -1:
fixed_file_name = "fixed_" + img_names[idx]
else:
fixed_file_name = img_names[idx][:pos_s + 1] + "fixed_" + img_names[idx][pos_s + 1:]
cv.imwrite(fixed_file_name, timelapser.getDst())
else:
blender.feed(cv.UMat(image_warped_s), mask_warped, corners[idx])
if not timelapse:
result = None
result_mask = None
result, result_mask = blender.blend(result, result_mask)
cv.imwrite(result_name, result)
zoom_x = 600.0 / result.shape[1]
dst = cv.normalize(src=result, dst=None, alpha=255., norm_type=cv.NORM_MINMAX, dtype=cv.CV_8U)
dst = cv.resize(dst, dsize=None, fx=zoom_x, fy=zoom_x)
cv.imshow("Stitched Image:",dst)
cv.waitKey(0)
# closing all open windows
cv.destroyAllWindows()
print("Done")

title: In python, cv2.imread returns a vague error message when the file is not found
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.2
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu Linux
- Compiler => Python
When the file is not found, I get the message
`cv2.error: OpenCV(3.4.2) /io/opencv/modules/imgproc/src/color.hpp:253: error: (-215:Assertion failed) VScn::contains(scn) && VDcn::contains(dcn) && VDepth::contains(depth) in function 'CvtHelper'`
This might be an appropriate error, but it is not helpful to the person that receives it
Another opinion: https://old.reddit.com/r/learnprogramming/comments/fr7ilb/opinion_there_should_be_an_initiative_to_make/flu9bps/
"You're right, the check should be explicit, and the user should be notified earlier, before the assertion fails, with a much clearer and intuitive error message."
```
import cv2
cv2.imread('sample-a.jpg')
```
The file 'sample-a.jpg' cannot be in the same directory as the python script
answers.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found solution

title: OpenCV stuck in getCoreTlsData
body:
OpenCV version: 4.5.5
Operating System / Platform: Microsoft Windows 10 Enterprise 2016 LTSB
Precompiled OpenCV world dll
We have a thread that is repeatedly calling BackgroundSubtractorMOG2::apply. After running for about 5 days it got stuck like below. I cannot reproduce this issue, but it has happened a few times now. I have a minidump available if needed.
getCoreTlsData calls ntdll!RtlpLowFragHeapAllocFromContext, and then WinDbg claims the current frame is ntdll!NtYieldExecution. There is some lazy initialization going on in getCoreTlsData, but as the process was already running for multiple days, I don't see how memory needs to be allocated. Is it a deadlock with some OS lock maybe (just guessing)?

title: SoftmaxInt8 is implemented but ONNX importer does not parse QLinearSoftmax
body:
[`QLinearSoftmax`](https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/blob/main/docs/ContribOperators.md#com.microsoft.QLinearSoftmax) is an operator from domain `com.microsoft`, which is not part of the standard ONNX opset. We have `SoftmaxInt8` implemented in https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/4.x/modules/dnn/src/int8layers/softmax_layer.cpp and `QLinearSoftmax` should be mapped to `SoftmaxInt8`, but there is no parser for `QLinearSoftmax` in the [ONNX importer](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/4.x/modules/dnn/src/onnx/onnx_importer.cpp).
Model is attached here [qlinearsoftmax.zip](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/files/11499121/qlinearsoftmax.zip). Network arch is as below:
![image](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/17219438/97e8b034-16c6-4ab7-9060-f8abefc341ca)

title: OpenGL support (drawing functions).
body:
As the option of using OpenGL based display exists ( cv::WINDOW_OPENGL) , it's more convenient to have the drawing primitives as well.
For now, displaying an image using opengl support means creating a  callback function and using glu.h or  gut.h for text , point , line ...  rendering.
I think it's more convenient to have for example cv::ogl::putText , cv::ogl::Line , cv::ogl::Rectangle ...etc

title: Can not import Opencv-android-sdk 4.0.1 into android studio
body:
I tried to import opencv-android-sdk version 4.0.1 into Android Studio but not successful.
Android Studio can not recognize library name.
I wrote lib name manually, but when build it appeared error: 'Cannot resolve symbol styleable'
I resolved by following the solution:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54206335/android-studio-and-opencv-compilation-error-cannot-resolve-symbol-styleable
But when run again, error is: "OpenCV was not initialised correctly. Application will be shutdown"
Can anyone help me the solution?
Thank you very much!

title: Problem compiling opencv3.2 on windows whit cmake and visual studio
body:
Hi everyone,
I would like to compile opencv 3.2 on windows 7 (64bit) with cmake and visual studio.
When I start compiling additional modules on cmake I return a
"C: \ Opencv \ opencv-build \ CMakeFiles \ CMakeTmp \ CheckIncludeFile.c (1) error: fatal error C1083: Can not open file include: 'pthread.h': No Such file or directory [C: \ Opencv \ opencv-build \ CMakeFiles \ CMakeTmp \ cmTC_6ba2c.vcxproj]"
How can I fix this problem? Thank you in advance

title: Is cvPOSIT function abandoned by opencv4.0.1?
body:
Thank you for your fabulous work on the version updating of opencv!
When I use opencv4.0.1 for 3D construction, I can't find the cvPOSIT function, which could use in opencv2.4.11, and I can't find any function that does what this function does (solvePNP is similar to it but different, and I can just find it in opencv\sources\modules\calib3d\src\calib3d_c_api.h, but not in build files, and can't use it). Could you please tell me which function has replaced it?
Hoping to hearing from you, and Thanks very much for your help!

title: Propose a new retrieval mode
body:
Following the exemple given here https://forum.opencv.org/t/issue-with-precision-of-cv2-findcontours/7947/3 , one can see that cv2.findContours is not able to separate an object and hole that are only one pixel apart. This generate issues when building a label array using the solution given here https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71034691/label-associated-to-findcontour-with-opencv that is then used with skimage.measure.regionprops (for which an issue has been opened here https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/21587)
As stated by @[crackwitz](https://github.com/crackwitz) the idea would be to propose a retrieval mode that follow the edges of the image instead of the center of the pixel.

title: pow of Mat with negative elements
body:
I've tried to convert some codes from MATLAB to c++ with OpenCV but I got stuck in this line :
MATLAB code :
`t3 = real((ISimMatrix .* QSimMatrix) .^ 0.02)`
I re implemented this line in c++ with:
```
cv::Mat t2, t3;
t2 = ISimMatrix.mul(QSimMatrix);
cv::pow(t2, lambda, t3); 
```
both `ISimMatrix `and `QSimMatrix` are 2D double matrix that might contain negative numbers, The problem is when the multiple of them power to 0.02, In MATLAB result is 2D complex matrix but in OpenCV result is `Mat_<double>` witch some elements are `nan` that cause all next calculations go wrong.
How can I implement `pow `function  that support negative power ?
OpenCV => 4.4
Operating System / Platform => Windows 64 Bit
Compiler => Visual Studio 2019

title: warning: ../../lib/libopencv_core.so, needed by ./lib/libopencv_highgui.so, not found (try using -rpath or -rpath-link)
body:
I have built the opencv project with cmake without errors. But when I compiled my project, I got warnings like this:
warning: ../../lib/libopencv_core.so, needed by ./lib/libopencv_highgui.so, not found (try using -rpath or -rpath-link)
I copyed the opencv library to the current folder and configed the environment. Then I run it and got the following error:
error while loading shared libraries: ../../lib/libopencv_videoio.so
I found it is because the compiler options in these files called link.txt. They are in their own folder like build/modules/*/CMakeFiles/opencv_*.dir. For example, the link.txt of highgui is in build/modules/highgui/CMakeFiles/opencv_highgui.dir/. And the build folder is built by cmake-gui. These files is writed like this:
g++  -fPIC    -fsigned-char -W -Wall -Werror=return-type -Werror=non-virtual-dtor -Werror=address -Werror=sequence-point -Wformat -Werror=format-security -Wmissing-declarations -Wundef -Winit-self -Wpointer-arith -Wshadow -Wsign-promo -Wuninitialized -Winit-self -Wno-narrowing -Wno-delete-non-virtual-dtor -Wno-comment -fdiagnostics-show-option -fomit-frame-pointer -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections  -fvisibility=hidden -fvisibility-inlines-hidden -Wno-deprecated-declarations -O3 -DNDEBUG  -DNDEBUG -lpthread -ldl -lm -lrt -Wl,-rpath-link=./lib -Wl,-rpath=./lib -shared  -o ../../lib/libopencv_highgui.so CMakeFiles/opencv_highgui.dir/src/window.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/opencv_highgui.dir/src/roiSelector.cpp.obj ../../lib/libopencv_videoio.so ../../lib/libopencv_imgcodecs.so ../../lib/libopencv_imgproc.so ../../lib/libopencv_core.so

title: Problem with convexityDefects
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => :grey_question:
- Operating System / Platform => :grey_question:
- Compiler => :grey_question:
#####  Hello, I'm trying to develop a shape detection app and while it is working as expected on most images, some images appear to fail with an incorrect number of defects from method convexityDefects
In the attached image:
1. The red lines show the contours found. There are four. The smallest 3 are ignored. (These contours look OK)
2. The light blue line shows the approximated polygon for the largest contour using approxPolyDP(). This also looks correct
3. The dark blue line shows hull covering approximated polygon. This also looks correct.
The problem is that convexityDefects() returns only two defects between the hull and polygon and the "distance" value in each result is far too low.(From inspection there should be about 8-10 defects)
The algorithm used is:
1. Color filter the image shown to a monochrome image, (MI) using "inRange" .
2. Dilate MI using "morphologyEx" to remove noise
3. use "Canny" against MI to produce a black background with white 'edges/contours'
4. Use "findContours" to detect the contour set. (There are four) Ignore the 3 smallest
5. Use "approxPolyDP()" to produce an approximated contour. (AC)
6. Use "convexHull" against AC to get the hull
7. Use "convexityDefects" the retrieve the defect list. (Only two defects are yielded!!)
![screenshot_thermalimage](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3505461/35466391-4961b050-02b8-11e8-9450-e6c3e385a3ae.png)

title: [SOS]When i compiled the Smoothing Demo，its Window showed orignal image .WHY?
highgui: wayland: if dependency is missing, "NO" message will help to install them.
body:
- OpenCV 4.x branch ( https://github.com/opencv/opencv/commit/2cd330486ec4597eab49c1575fc4a6603f205a6a )
- Ubuntu 24.04
- GCC 13.2
Evenif dependency is missing, we cannnot know what dependency is missing.
https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/2cd330486ec4597eab49c1575fc4a6603f205a6a/CMakeLists.txt#L1368-L1384
It is better to show "NO" if dependency libraries are missing.
```diff
- if(HAVE_WAYLAND_CLIENT)
-   status("    Wayland Client:" "YES (ver ${WAYLAND_CLIENT_VERSION})")
- endif()
+ status("    Wayland Client:" HAVE_WAYLAND_CLIENT THEN "YES (ver ${WAYLAND_CLIENT_VERSION})" ELSE "NO")
```
Case) WITH_WAYLAND is not set.
```plaintext
  GUI:                           NO
    GTK+:                        NO
    VTK support:                 NO
```
Case) `WITH_WAYLAND` is set, and all dependencies are installed.
```plaintext
  GUI:                           Wayland
    Wayland:                     (Experimental) YES
      Wayland Client:            YES (ver 1.22.0)
      Wayland Cursor:            YES (ver 1.22.0)
      Wayland Protocols:         YES (ver 1.34)
      Xkbcommon:                 YES (ver 1.6.0)
      Wayland EGL(Option):       NO
    GTK+:                        NO
    VTK support:                 NO
```
Case) `WITH_WAYLAND` is set, but `Xkbcommon` is missing, so Wayland backend is disabled.
```plaintext
  GUI:                           NO
    Wayland:                     NO
      Wayland Client:            YES (ver 1.22.0)
      Wayland Cursor:            YES (ver 1.22.0)
      Wayland Protocols:         YES (ver 1.34)
      Xkbcommon:                 NO
      Wayland EGL(Option):       NO
    GTK+:                        NO
    VTK support:                 NO
```

title: Changing capture mode for webcam does not work on macOS
body:
OpenCV  python version: 4.7.0.72
Operating System / Platform:  macOS 11.7.3
Python version: 3.11.1
I'm trying to increase frame rate using MJPEG compression with my webcam by setting `CAP_PROP_FOURCC` to `cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*"MJPG")`. The call always returns `False`. No other error message. I use the default backend (`AVFOUNDATION`). Same call works with the _same_ camera on Windows (10) using `MSMF` backend.
Example code on macOS:
```python
import cv2
v = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
v.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FOURCC, cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*"MJPG"))
```
Just yields `False` on  the last function call. I looked in the source code for AVFOUNDATION and found that MJPG is not implemented, but a few other seems to be:
https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/86fa0308fc17f982cf9aa291b5e53e7b9e71e0be/modules/videoio/src/cap_avfoundation_mac.mm#L51
None of these modes (e.g. `cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*"YUYV")` works either, just returns `False` for all of them and makes no difference.
Running the same code on Windows yields `True` and my frame rate jumps from ~15 FPS to ~30 FPS as expected.

title: Different Yolo performing time in different versions
body:
I have accesses to 2 servers with identical hardware:
Frist:
CPU -> AMD EPYC 7551P 32-Core
GPU -> 2 Nvidia Tesla V-100 16 GB
OS -> Windows Server 2016 Datacenter
GPU driver -> Nvidia Tesla V-100 441.22
CUDA -> 10.2.89
OpenCV -> 4.4.0
Compiler -> Visual Studio 2019
Enviroment -> Anaconda with Python 3.7
Second:
CPU -> AMD EPYC 7551P 32-Core
GPU -> 2 Nvidia Tesla V-100 16 GB
OS -> Centos 7
GPU driver -> Nvidia Tesla V-100  VGPU driver 430.30
CUDA -> 10.0.130
OpenCV -> 4.5.0-pre.
Compiler -> gcc 4.8.5 20150623
Enviroment -> Python 3.6 (just python enviroment)
When I perform object detection with the same code for the same videos, I get different fps for these servers.
I use darknet YoloV4 object detector model from AlexeyAB https://github.com/AlexeyAB/darknet
I also have the same thing with YoloV3-spp and YoloV3 models.
On the first server with input image size 416x416 - 36-38 fps
On the first server with input image size 608x608 - 16-17 fps
On the second server with input image size 416x416 - 62-63 fps
On the second server with input image size 608x608 - 37-38 fps
I don't understand if it is because of some performance improvement in new OpenCV version or it is because of some problems with version OpenCV for Windows.
I am attaching the code to reproduce the problem.
[OpenCVYolo.txt](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/files/5595121/OpenCVYolo.txt)

title: Convert numpy array elements into images
body:
Hello
Consider the object 'train_x' is a numpy array with dimension (10,28,28), can you please help me in converting these 10 array elements into 10 different images using opencv and name accordingly and store in a location, say "E:\\Images".
Any reference or example will be helpful.

title: Opencv installation in linux
body:
Sometime user feels a lot of problem while installing opencv package. This issue is about solving problem of installing opencv in linux operating system.
Should I work on it??

title: 'forward' method of cv2::dnn::net crashes during a test
body: I compiled OpenCV with CUDA by Visual Studio 2015 in Release mode successfully. Everything went well during building process and i added all libraries to a Qt project. When i set _PreferableBackend_ and _PreferableTarget_ to _cv::dnn::DNN_BACKEND_CUDA_ and _cv::dnn::DNN_TARGET_CUDA_ respectively, application crashes at _net.forward()_ and Qt writes _'The program has unexpectedly finished'_ to the output, though everything goes well by removing _PreferableBackend_ and _PreferableTarget_ .

title: Feature request for area taking into account pixel area of contour
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.4.1
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 64 Bit
- Compiler => 3.7.1
contourArea() gives area different to pixel count, since it considers the contour ideal that is without an area. In some applications, where pixels have considerable size in real life, e.g. satellite applications, this matters. Depending on the size of the connected component the percentual difference between the result from contourArea() to pixel count varies. The problem worsens, the smaller the connected component. In the attached example the pixel width is 400 m. For the class of components with area 1 to 10 km2 (7 to 62 pixels), contourArea underestimates the area on average by 44%.
This is a feature request to calculate the area taking the area of the contour itself as well as the inside into account.
![Heatmap](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54664318/82535584-f6da4a00-9b3e-11ea-8ca7-e5f9be19d00b.png)
```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import cv2
import pandas as pd
im = cv2.imread('Heatmap.png')
num_comp, comp_labels, comp_stats, comp_centroid = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(im[:,:,0], 8, cv2.CV_32S)
index_max = np.argsort(comp_stats[:,cv2.CC_STAT_AREA])
pixel_width_m = 400
pixel_area_km2 = pixel_width_m**2 / 10**6
sizes = np.array([0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000]) / pixel_area_km2
sizes_indices = [np.where(comp_stats[index_max,cv2.CC_STAT_AREA] >= sizes[i])[0][0] for i in range(5)]
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[0]:sizes_indices[1]][::-1].tolist() # class 0.1 to 1 km2
indices = index_max[sizes_indices[1]:sizes_indices[2]][::-1].tolist() # class 1 to 10 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[2]:sizes_indices[3]][::-1].tolist() # class 10 to 100 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[3]:sizes_indices[4]][::-1].tolist() # class 100 to 1000 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[4]:-1][::-1].tolist() # class > 1000 km2
df = pd.DataFrame(columns = ['area count', 'area', 'ratio areas'])
for index in indices:
    points = np.array(np.where(comp_labels == index)).T
    temp = np.zeros(comp_labels.shape).astype(np.uint8)
    temp[comp_labels == index] = 255
    _, contour, _ = cv2.findContours(temp,cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL,cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_NONE)
    area = cv2.contourArea(contour[0])
    df.loc[len(df)] = [len(points), area, area/len(points)]
df['area count'].hist()
df['area'].hist()
df['ratio areas'].hist()
df.describe().iloc[0:,0:]
```
![Heatmap](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54664318/80916628-cf6b3b00-8d51-11ea-93d0-bfbdcde5858f.png)
answers.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found solution

title: cv2.rectangle() works abnormally
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 4.1.1
- Operating System / Platform => Ubuntu 20.04
- Compiler => PyCharm
##### cv2.rectangle() works abnomally
I tried to draw bounding boxes of objects in an image.
At first, I can draw bounding boxes on the image successfully.
Then I copy the image and flip image and bounding boxes. This time I cannot draw any boxes on the image.
```python
def annotation_flip_test():
    dataset = Dataset('train')
    anno = '/home/user/work/DATA/VOC2007/test/JPEGImages/004538.jpg 215,35,308,224,14 41,77,403,367,12'
    image, bboxes = dataset.parse_annotation(annotation=anno)
    flip_img, flip_bboxes = np.copy(image), np.copy(bboxes)
    _, w, _ = flip_img.shape
    flip_img = flip_img[:, ::-1, :]   # flip image
    flip_bboxes[:, [0, 2]] = w - flip_bboxes[:, [2, 0]]   # flip bboxes

    print("========== original box ==========")
    show_image('original', image)  # Show original image
    show_image('original_with_bbox', image, bboxes)  # Show original image with bboxes

    print("========== flipped box ==========")
    show_image('flipped_with_bbox', flip_img, flip_bboxes)  # Show flipped image with bboxes, but only flipped image showed without out bboxes


def show_image(img_name, img, bboxes=None):
    if bboxes is None:
        cv2.imshow(img_name, img)
    else:
        for bbox in bboxes:
            print('bbox: ', bbox)
            cv2.rectangle(img, (bbox[0], bbox[1]), (bbox[2], bbox[3]), (0, 255, 0), 2)
            cv2.imshow(img_name, img)
        cv2.imshow(img_name, img)
    while cv2.waitKey(100) != 27:
        if cv2.getWindowProperty(img_name, cv2.WND_PROP_VISIBLE) <= 0:
            break
    cv2.destroyAllWindows()
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23485976/96924829-d4938200-1478-11eb-987c-ff0d3fa904d0.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23485976/96924878-e8d77f00-1478-11eb-88ec-30e41c5d377e.png)

title: Possible bug in cornerHarris
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.4.8.29
- Operating System / Platform => Windows10 64 Bit
- Compiler => python
a posible bug in harris corner detector:
this are inputs and outputs of a constant gradient to cornerHarris:
```
import cv2
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px

x = 10
aa = [[np.array(range(x))+2*i] for i in range(x)]
a = np.block(aa).reshape(x,x)
a = (a/np.max(a)*255).astype(np.uint8)
# a = 10*np.array(range(x**2)).reshape(x,x)
px.imshow(a).show()

res = cv2.cornerHarris(a.astype(np.uint8), 5, 5, 0.04)
px.imshow(res).show()
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16686924/74941093-202df180-53fb-11ea-9963-499c2b97e454.png)
- Boarder is wrong.
- As I saw in the source code, you are using the covariance matrix of the patch without subtracting the mean. Why is this? I can think of this as PCA without subtracting the mean- which can result in an error

title: Not sure why cuda isn't enabled
body:
Let's take it from the top, i had built opencv with cuda 7.5 and installed that because i couldn't figure out how to get cuda 8.0 to be found. I then rebuilt the project with cuda 7.5 and processor optimizations. I didn't install that build. Instead, I installed clang-5.0 and tried to build with that. Magically, cuda 8.0 was found. However, it ran into problems linking protobuff and libwebp. Since it required a bit tinkering to get clang to work, I cleared the build directory and restarted terminal. Now, only clang is found and neither cuda installation is found. If i scroll up, i see that it tells me that cuda can't be used due to clang incompatibilities. So, i deleted that section of the cmake file because cuda can be built with clang. It also appears that the section of the cmake file was targeted at mac users. Below is my cmake command
`cmake -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RELEASE -D CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local -D WITH_CUDA=ON -D ENABLE_FAST_MATH=1 -D CUDA_FAST_MATH=1 -D WITH_CUBLAS=1 -D INSTALL_PYTHON_EXAMPLES=ON -D OPENCV_EXTRA_MODULES_PATH=../../opencv_contrib/modules -D BUILD_EXAMPLES=ON -D WITH_TBB=ON -D WITH_OPENMP=ON -D WITH_IPP=ON -D ENABLE_AVX=ON -D ENABLE_AVX2=ON -D ENABLE_POPCNT=ON -D ENABLE_SSE41=ON -D ENABLE_SSE42=ON -D ENABLE_SSSE3=ON -D_CMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_PREFIX=llvm -D CUDA_TOOLKIT_ROOT_DIR=/usr/local/cuda -DCMAKE_C_COMPILER=/usr/bin/clang -DCMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=clang++ ..`

title: 3D Reconstruction Task: In video processing tasks, the issue of slow detection speed of Aruco under low light, high resolution, and small target detection conditions.
body:
In video processing tasks, we encountered a problem where the detection speed of Aruco was very slow under the conditions described in the title. Through testing under different lighting conditions, we found that while the recognition accuracy of Aruco remained high, the detection speed was significantly slowed down. After analyzing the detection principle of Aruco, I concluded that the main factor affecting the speed was the noise caused by low lighting conditions.
To address this issue, I believe a fast preprocessing function module can be introduced to process the images being detected, improving Aruco's detection speed. This would help meet the real-time requirements of video processing tasks, while also enhancing robustness for tasks under normal lighting conditions.
Through a series of experiments, I have found some solutions to address the performance issues with speed, and I would like to raise this issue to spark discussion within the community, as well as to improve the current methods.
**Solution 1: AI Denoising** Denoising the image can reduce the noise to some extent and thus speed up detection. However, applying an AI denoise model takes 1-2 seconds, which is still unacceptable in real-time video processing tasks.
**Solution 2: YOLO + Aruco** By training YOLO on a large dataset of Aruco markers, we can still achieve fast detection and recognition in low-light environments. However, this method has a high cost: it requires organizing the dataset, training YOLO, and extracting the four corner coordinates of the quadrilateral through detection. It also requires extensive custom modifications to the original Aruco functions to obtain the ordered sequence of corner coordinates. The detection time is around 100ms per frame. Since the implementation cost of the YOLO method is high and the OpenCV community primarily uses traditional image processing methods for Aruco, we are considering alternative solutions, with this one as a backup.
**Solution 3: Video Tracking Points During video capture** I noticed that Aruco markers have a continuous property, meaning that the position of Aruco in the current frame doesn't move significantly in the next frame. First, Aruco markers are small target detections, which means that most of the other areas in the image are irrelevant, often filled with noise. By leveraging this continuity, we can focus detection on certain areas. The green region represents the detected area in the current frame, and the red region extends outward to form a reference for the next frame. Reducing the area can significantly speed up Aruco code detection. We need to save the positions of different Aruco markers in the previous frame and then define a small region for detection in the next frame, of course with appropriate boundary conditions.
**Solution 4: Canny edge detection algorithm combined with a dilation kernel** Solution 4 is based on improvements made to Solution 3. We found that both solutions focus on detecting areas of interest in the image and discarding most of the irrelevant regions to speed up Aruco detection. However, Solution 3 is relatively complex, involving read/write operations for some Aruco points. Additionally, video processing tasks often require multi-threading, and frames in the sequence might not be processed in the same thread. This requires further modifications to the thread handling code, giving rise to Solution 4.
We found that Aruco markers in images have clear boundary conditions. Based on this, we can use the Canny edge detection algorithm combined with a dilation kernel to expand the area of interest. However, it is important to note that this method works best in simple backgrounds; if the environment is too complex, the results may not be as good as those achieved with a YOLO-trained model. This is a key point I hope to discuss, as this acceleration method works well only in scenarios with less complex backgrounds. It meets the needs of my current project, but I invite the community to discuss and improve upon this approach.

![图片2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/593f22b1-ebff-4d2e-bbd6-5ebb18141f95)
![2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f19a5ef2-de95-4460-b2e6-36e28b4ed081)
![edge_guided_smoothing_no_salt_pepper](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/68571434-1f66-4722-a227-426b8d7c2b12)

title: Two models Mask RCNN models trained with different image size have same inference speed with OpenCV DNN
body:
### System information (version)
- OpenCV =>  4.5.0
- Operating System / Platform => Mac OS 10.13.5 64 bits
- Compiler => clang with qmake
**CONTEXT**
I trained a custom MASK RCNN using Tensorflow Object Detection API to detect areas on the face. My input images are 480x480 but I chose to resize them to 240x240 so that the training can be quicker.
**ISSUE**
I did the inference in C++ on several computer using OpenCV DNN in another project and I have very different speed results (each computer are very different so nothing to be concerned about so far, again the predictions are good) but I have an inference time of around 2.5 seconds on the worst one (no GPU) which is too long for my purpose.
**WHAT I DID**
I trained another time my MASK RCNN model using this time 120x120 images as input. The training went around 3 times faster and the results are good enough (performing around 7% worst than the first one which is okay for me).
**PROBLEM**
When running the inference with OpenCV the inference is only less than 10ms faster (~2.5s total time) when in my opinion it should be much faster seeing how fast the training was.
I am feeding my network with a blob created such as :
` blob = cv::dnn::blobFromImage(image, 1.0, cv::Size(blob_height, blob_width), cv::Scalar(), true, false);`
` net_.setInput(blob, "image_tensor");`
` this->net_.forward(outputs_, outNames);`
with `blob_height` and `blob_width` being 120 or 240 depending on the model and my image is a cv::Mat of size (480,480).
The time I am checking is the forward time.
I am wondering if there is an explanation why there is so few improvement between the two models. Is the function blobFromImage return a tensor with the same kind shape as the input image or something? I'd appreciate any help helping me to understand this or providing tips to fasten the process!

title: STMAP exporter
body:
STmaps[1][2] are quite popular in many pipelines but it seems hard to replicate what it is expected to be processed by other software like Natron, Blender, Nuke, etc.
It seems that exporting it with `initUndistortRectifyMap` it will not have the same effect in these software as OpenCV `initUndistortRectifyMap` + `remap` also using the same interpolation and datatype precision.
It is going always to have substantial visual difference.
[1]https://benmcewan.com/blog/2020/02/02/demystifying-st-maps/
[²]https://natron.readthedocs.io/en/rb-2.5/plugins/net.sf.openfx.STMap.html
It could be partially connected to https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/22120 as we need a direct mapping and not the inverse one expected by `remap`

title: CommandLineParser parse string arguments as literally "true" in 3.4.1
body:
##### System information (version)
I can confirm this problem on both the platforms:
Linux
- OpenCV => 3.4.1
- Operating System / Platform => Fedora 28 64 Bit
- Compiler => gcc (GCC) 8.1.1 20180502 (Red Hat 8.1.1-1)
Windows
- OpenCV => 3.4.1
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 64 Bit
- Compiler => Visual Studio 2015
In short, CommandLineParser cannot parse string on the command line correctly.
I think the "Steps to reproduce" would have a clear description on this.
BTW: **I believe this is the cause that the OpenCV apps like opencv_createsamples, opencv_annotation, opencv_traincascade will not work out of box**
Minimal working file:
```
// demo.cpp
#include <opencv2/opencv.hpp>
#include <iostream>

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    const cv::String keys = "{path p         |/the/default/path     | path to file         }";
    cv::CommandLineParser parser(argc, argv, keys);
    std::string str_path = parser.get<std::string>("path");
    std::cout << "path is " << str_path << std::endl;
    return 0;
}
```
I use this CMakeLists.txt:
```
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 2.8)
project( demo )
find_package( OpenCV REQUIRED )
include_directories( ${OpenCV_INCLUDE_DIRS} )
add_executable( demo demo.cpp)
target_link_libraries( demo ${OpenCV_LIBS} )
```
Make:
```
cmake .
make
```
Run (demonstrating on my Linux host):
```
$ ./demo
path is /the/default/path
```
The default value is correct, but it won't parse the string I pass it through the command line at all!
```
$ ./demo -p "/home/user/images"
path is true
```
`path is true`? But `path is /home/user/images` is expected!

title: getConvertScaleFunc(): equal treatment of underlying convertors
body:
`cv::cvtScale32s()` is defined as:
```
DEF_CVT_SCALE_FUNC(32s, cvt_64f, int, int, double)
```
which makes sense to achieve high accurcy (to pass `Core_Transform*` tests?).
But why only `cvtScale32s()` uses `double`, while the rest conversion functions utilizes `float`? From equal-treatment point of view, I expect `cvtScale32s()` to utilize `float`, or all rest convertors `cvtScale*()` to utilize `double` as well.
What do you think?

title: eigen2cv data type conversion
body:
OpenCV 4.x
Ubuntu 20.04
g++  9.4.0
I would like to request the possibility to pass a [data type](https://docs.opencv.org/3.4/d1/d1b/group__core__hal__interface.html)  such as `CV_32F` to the [`eigen2cv()`](https://docs.opencv.org/4.8.0/d0/daf/group__core__eigen.html#ga7d024b90a359c852cce5dff47ebc77a4) function. Currently, this function converts matrices to the `CV_64F` data type. This behavior limits flexibility, especially in applications where CV_32F is preferred due to its lower memory footprint and faster processing times.
The ability to directly convert Eigen matrices to `CV_32F` or any data type would be highly beneficial for performance-sensitive applications, such as real-time image processing and computer vision tasks in robotics.
There has been some discussion [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72976900/cvgetperspectivetransform-throws-error-after-cveigen2cv)

title: imgcodecs: jpeg: refactoring JpegEncoder with/without libjpeg turbo

However old codes for libjpeg  are left, so it is not easy to read.

For non-continuous source, I think updating to use Mat::ptr() insted of Mat::data + bias is better way.
```diff
- uchar *data = img.data + img.step*y, *ptr = data;
+ uchar *data = const_cast<uchar*>(img.ptr<uchar>(y));
```

title: Load RTree model file
estimateAffinePartial2D without scaling
body:
I use [estimateAffinePartial2D](https://docs.opencv.org/4.x/d9/d0c/group__calib3d.html#gad767faff73e9cbd8b9d92b955b50062d) function to stabilize a set of images of a fixed camera dedicated to coastline monitoring. The camera zoom is fixed. There are small camera movements due to for example the thermal dilation of the structure on which the camera is installed.
The stabilization result with estimateAffinePartial2D is quite good, but it could be really better if scaling factor was not computed and left to 1, as it would better suit the camera configuration.
It would be great to have a boolean parameter in estimateAffinePartial2D, in which the user can specifiy if scaling factor has to be computed or not. Otherwise, it might be more simple to implement an other function called for example 'estimateRigidBody2D' that would compute a rigid body (translation + rotation) transformation from matching points.

On Windows (Anaconda, conda-forge channel, `opencv=4.5.3=py39hcbf5309_1`), `cv2.watershed` accepts `markers` arguments of `dtype==int`. However, the same fails on Linux (python-3.9.7:slim docker image on Ubuntu 20.04) unless `dtype==np.int32`. Both OSes are 64-bit. See Steps to reproduce.

title: Correct averaging of colors
body:
The fact that averaging vectors in [sRGB color space][1] doesn't yield perceptually nice results is hinted at in [the video "Computer color is broken"][2] and in [Wikipedia's explanation of the sRGB transfer function][3].
Does [`resize()`][4] make this mistake when interpolating colors (combining several colors into one pixel when downscaling and interpolating new intermediate pixels when upscaling)?
If it interpolates colors correctly, where in the code does it apply the transfer function? The problem is that the documentation doesn't say so.
If it interpolates colors wrong, I wonder what transfer functions I should apply (in Python code) to the image **before and after** `resize()` for better color averaging ([`colour.models.eotf_sRGB`](https://colour.readthedocs.io/en/develop/generated/colour.models.eotf_sRGB.html#colour.models.eotf_sRGB) before and [`colour.models.eotf_inverse_sRGB`](https://colour.readthedocs.io/en/develop/generated/colour.models.eotf_inverse_sRGB.html#colour.models.eotf_inverse_sRGB) after or the other way around or something else), and whether that would be perfect or only an approximation (similarly to how [gamma correction with gamma=2.2 is only an approximation of the sRGB transfer function][3]).
The documentation of `resize()` should mention it and maybe do it (depending on additional input arguments).
In general, does OpenCV assume the sRGB color space in such cases where the color space influences the results? Maybe the documentation should mention that.
[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SRGB
[2]: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LKnqECcg6Gw
[3]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SRGB#Transfer_function_(%22gamma%22)
[4]: https://docs.opencv.org/4.5.5/da/d54/group__imgproc__transform.html#ga47a974309e9102f5f08231edc7e7529d

title: 5.x : ts : getTypeName() risks out-of-bounds memory access(Low risk).
body:
OpenCV version: 5.x ( 1d18aba587f1e4df1d6c00801702573b95bb1ebe )
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 23.10
Compiler & compiler version: GCC 12.3.0
When `type` is `CV_16BF`, `CV_Bool`, `CV_64U`, `CV_64S` or `CV_32U`, out-of-boundary memory access will be occured.
https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/1d18aba587f1e4df1d6c00801702573b95bb1ebe/modules/ts/src/ts_func.cpp#L11-L15
This is a precautionary measure and there is no reproducible code.
`ts.hpp` provides this function prototype definition.
So if anyone use in test code, it may stop test.
https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/1d18aba587f1e4df1d6c00801702573b95bb1ebe/modules/ts/include/opencv2/ts.hpp#L279-L280

title: Training cascade is too slow if all negative files are small pictures.
body:
Before training cascade, all positive files are transformed into a single vec file, but negative files are still spread on disk.
If there are many small negative files(millions), training will be very slow. The program can't sample many negative samples from one negative file, so it keep reading all small files from disk. But the disk and file system can't afford the cost of reading many small files. IO becomes the bottleneck of training speed.
Is it possible to transform or concat all negative files into one big file before training (just like positives)?

title: Compilation errors with g++-8 for OpenCV 4.3.0 with opencv_contrib (xfeatures2d namespace conflict)
cv2.VideoCapture on GIF files break after Pyinstaller
body:
Sorry if this is a bug generated by pyinstaller, I don't have enough knowledge to know who is responsible for this bug.
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => Tested in 4.1.2.30 and 3.4.8.29
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 Windows 64 Bit
- Compiler =>Python 3.6.9
- PyInstaller => 3.5
Using cv2.VideoCapture with both **mp4** and **gif** files as input work perfectly before compiling a .exe using pyinstaller. Trying the same code in the .exe generated by pyinstaller still work for **mp4** files, but for **gif** files it throw this error:
````
[ERROR:0] VIDEOIO(cvCreateFileCapture_Images(filename.c_str())): raised OpenCV exception:

OpenCV(3.4.8) C:\projects\opencv-python\opencv\modules\videoio\src\cap_images.cpp:246: error: (-5:Bad argument) CAP_IMAGES: can't find starting number (in the name of file): E:\Downloads\gifs\mygif.gif in function 'cv::icvExtractPattern'
````
Write a .py file with this code:
````
import cv2

def GetFPS(pathVideo):
    cam = cv2.VideoCapture(pathVideo)
    print("Input FPS: " + str(cam.get(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS)))

GetFPS("{Full path to mp4 file}")
GetFPS("{Full path to gif file}")
````
Now run the code:
`pyinstaller myfile.py`
Executing the final .exe should run the mp4 just fine, but thow the error in the gif file.

title: Add "initUndistortRectifyMap()" but for a sparse array of points
body:
Hi.
I have a use-case which requires to know non-rectified float positions for a small set of rectified float positions. Points are rectified from a generic distortion (not necessarily fisheye). As far as I know, there is no easy/fast way to do it using OpenCV functions.
Inspired by [`cv::initUndistortRectifyMap()`](https://docs.opencv.org/4.8.0/d9/d0c/group__calib3d.html#ga7dfb72c9cf9780a347fbe3d1c47e5d5a) and [`cv::fisheye::distortPoints()`](https://docs.opencv.org/4.8.0/db/d58/group__calib3d__fisheye.html#ga75d8877a98e38d0b29b6892c5f8d7765), I could imagine an additional function like:
```cpp
void cv::distortPoints(InputArray cameraMatrix,
                       InputArray distCoeffs,
                       InputArray R,
                       InputArray newCameraMatrix,
                       InputArray undistorted,
                       OutputArray distorted
                      ) 
```
As of today, we can either:
* Use `initUndistortRectifyMap()` and then lookup the coordinates for the few points we need to know ([see example here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/20784849/2291710)). This has the drawback of pre-calculating a dense matrix of coordinates when only a minority will be used, and it doesn't work for floating input coordinates (requires implementation bilinear interpolation).
* Re-implement the equations described in the documentation and apply them to our points ([see example here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/24231047/2291710)). It was pretty straightforward in the days of Opencv 2.4, but the formula now supports additional distortion parameters and has become non-trivial. Nor does it allow you to take advantage of the vectorization optimizations implemented by OpenCV.
I'm apparently not the 1st one to have such need, and I think it would be a useful addition to OpenCV.

title: contourArea gives area very different to pixel count
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.4.1
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 64 Bit
- Compiler => 3.7.1
contourArea gives area different to pixel count. Depending on the size of the connected component this is very different. The problem worsens, the smaller the connected component. In the attached example the pixel width is 400 m. For the class of components with area 1 to 10 km2 (7 to 62 pixels), contourArea underestimates the area on average by 44%.
```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import cv2
import pandas as pd
im = cv2.imread('Heatmap.png')
num_comp, comp_labels, comp_stats, comp_centroid = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(im[:,:,0], 8, cv2.CV_32S)
index_max = np.argsort(comp_stats[:,cv2.CC_STAT_AREA])
pixel_width_m = 400
pixel_area_km2 = pixel_width_m**2 / 10**6
sizes = np.array([0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000]) / pixel_area_km2
sizes_indices = [np.where(comp_stats[index_max,cv2.CC_STAT_AREA] >= sizes[i])[0][0] for i in range(5)]
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[0]:sizes_indices[1]][::-1].tolist() # class 0.1 to 1 km2
indices = index_max[sizes_indices[1]:sizes_indices[2]][::-1].tolist() # class 1 to 10 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[2]:sizes_indices[3]][::-1].tolist() # class 10 to 100 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[3]:sizes_indices[4]][::-1].tolist() # class 100 to 1000 km2
#indices = index_max[sizes_indices[4]:-1][::-1].tolist() # class > 1000 km2
df = pd.DataFrame(columns = ['area count', 'area', 'ratio areas'])
for index in indices:
    points = np.array(np.where(comp_labels == index)).T
    temp = np.zeros(comp_labels.shape).astype(np.uint8)
    temp[comp_labels == index] = 255
    _, contour, _ = cv2.findContours(temp,cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL,cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_NONE)
    area = cv2.contourArea(contour[0])
    df.loc[len(df)] = [len(points), area, area/len(points)]
df['area count'].hist()
df['area'].hist()
df['ratio areas'].hist()
df.describe().iloc[0:,0:]
```
![Heatmap](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54664318/80916628-cf6b3b00-8d51-11ea-93d0-bfbdcde5858f.png)
answers.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found solution

[  FAILED  ] Size_Depth_Channels_split.split/31, where GetParam() = (1920x1080, 32FC1, 3)
[  FAILED  ] Size_Depth_Channels_split.split/32, where GetParam() = (1920x1080, 32FC1, 4)
[  FAILED  ] Size_Depth_Channels_split.split/42, where GetParam() = (127x61, 32FC1, 2)
[  FAILED  ] Size_Depth_Channels_split.split/43, where GetParam() = (127x61, 32FC1, 3)
[  FAILED  ] Size_Depth_Channels_split.split/44, where GetParam() = (127x61, 32FC1, 4)

12 FAILED TESTS
```
### Additional description
- I traced the cause, and realized the situation is similar what I experienced in #6536
- For example, the error value is
- **expected** value is 0.0009918212890625 which is 0x3A820000
- actual value is 0.00099554657936096191 which is 0x3A82**7D**00
- I dumped the array, and the array does have the value 0x3A827D00
```
ubuntu@pine64:~/opencv/build$ xxd -s 1078 -e -g4 original.bmp | grep -i ' 3a82'
00213336: 4291892d 43611081 3a827d00 42c83f23  -..B..aC.}.:#?.B
```
- I dumped the same array from windows machine, and realized the array already has different value.
```
ubuntu@pine64:~/opencv/build$ xxd -e -g4 -s1078 win32.bmp | grep -i 3a820000
00213336: 4291892c 43611081 3a820000 42c83f23  ,..B..aC...:#?.B
```
- Now, since the operation is **split** no operation is applied against the values
- Cause of the cause, comes from modules/core/src/rand.cpp
``` c++
 arr[i+0] = f[0]*p[i+0][0] + p[i+0][1];
 arr[i+1] = f[1]*p[i+1][0] + p[i+1][1];
 arr[i+2] = f[2]*p[i+2][0] + p[i+2][1];
 arr[i+3] = f[3]*p[i+3][0] + p[i+3][1];
```
- When the multiply of f[0]*p[i+0][0] and (-p[i+0][1]) becomes very similar value, rounding error becomes huge.
### Question
- For the fix in #6536, I loosen the threshold for particular tests, but same approach seems difficult for opencv_perf_core, since there is no 'test_case' variable.
- I'd like to ask a help to which way to go
- loosen the threshold in perf_split.cpp (probably from 1e-12 to 1e-5, which seems huge to me)
- use volatile in rand.cpp to avoid using fmadd
- force every platform to use fused multiply and add ( I don't think this choise is possible )
Any suggestion ?

title: The current Mat::at has no constraints for the data type of different channels as the source.
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
The current Mat::at has no constraints for the data type of different channels as the source. And, it will cause a little confusion in some code practices.
For example:
``` c++
cv::Mat img(rows, cols, CV_64F);
```
The number of channels of `img` is 1, however, the following operations are still correct since the number of channels of `cv::Vec3d` is 3.
``` c++
img.at<cv::Vec3d>(0, 0)[0] = 255.0;
img.at<cv::Vec3d>(0, 0)[1] = 255.0;
img.at<cv::Vec3d>(0, 0)[2] = 255.0;
```
The [] operation in cv::Vec limits the number of channels, but it cannot limit the converted ones in this case.
**I personally feel that the better way is to add some assertions or warnings that appear during compile time.**
Related PR: https://github.com/opencv/opencv/pull/22733

// core_example.cpp

    // Create two 3x3 matrices filled with 1.0 and 2.0 respectively
    cv::Mat mat1 = cv::Mat::ones(3, 3, CV_32FC1) * 1.0f; // 3x3 matrix filled with 1.0

title: Use of omp_set_dynamic() creates nonsensical OpenMP behaviour, using only 1 thread
body:
All Linux systems, probably more, see `libomp` code.
@alalek I think the behaviour introduced in
* https://github.com/opencv/opencv/pull/12796
is bad because of how [`OMP_DYNAMIC`] is implemented. It makes that OpenMP does not use all available cores.
I noticed that when starting my C++ app, only 1 thread is used, even though the system is idle.
* On other machines with the same spec, running the same binary, all cores were used.
* Running the app a few minutes later made a couple more threads used.
* The more I waited, the more performant the app got.
Setting e.g. `OMP_NUM_THREADS=100` did not help.
I took a wild guess and observed the observed parallelism against the 15-minute load average of the machine:
```
15 min load average
    2.55 -> 10 threads
    4.64 -> 8 threads
    4.93 -> 7 threads
```
My program's code path in question doesn't even use, but only link/load OpenCV.
I eventually found the implementation: [`libgomp/config/linux/proc.c`](https://github.com/gcc-mirror/gcc/blob/10cb3336ba1ac89b258f627222e668b023a6d3d4/libgomp/config/linux/proc.c#L180-L188)
```c
/* When OMP_DYNAMIC is set, at thread launch determine the number of
   threads we should spawn for this team.  */
/* ??? I have no idea what best practice for this is.  Surely some
   function of the number of processors that are *still* online and
   the load average.  Here I use the number of processors online
   minus the 15 minute load average.  */

unsigned
gomp_dynamic_max_threads (void) {
///
    return n_onln - loadavg;
```
## This is really bad
* Because of this logic, your app will use only 1 thread, even though the system is completely idle _now_, just because it was busy 10 minutes ago.
* The dynamic limit is determined _at process start_ (loading time), and fixed forever.
* So started programs stay slow _forever_ if they were started at a time 5 minutes after the system was busy.
* It means a server can never achieve full utilisation when working down a queue of jobs.
* Say you have 8 cores, and a queue of N jobs to process, each of which takes 15 minutes full-CPU.
* The first jobs starts at 0 15-min-utilisation, thus using all cores.
* The next job starts, using only 1 core.
* The next job starts, using only 7 cores.
* The next job starts, using only 1 cores.
* The next job starts, using only 7 cores.
* In the long run, the **server uses only half of its cores on average**.
* It makes performance behaviour completely irreproducible.
* This should not be on by default.
OpenCV isn't really to blame here, because the [docs of `OMP_DYNAMIC`](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/libgomp/OMP_005fDYNAMIC.html) point none of this out. You have to read the implementation to learn it.
## Solution
I petition OpenCV to disable this logic by default, setting `OPENCV_FOR_OPENMP_DYNAMIC_DISABLE=1` by default.
```sh
stress-ng --cpu 0
# wait 15 minutes, then Ctrl+C

# Now run your app that links OpenCV. 
# It will use only 1 thread for the next 10 minutes.
```
